On reflection, furl objects should not be used as dictionary keys. They're mutable.

From Python 3's datamodel documentation

```
If a class defines mutable objects and implements an __eq__() method, it should not implement __hash__(), since the implementation of hashable collections requires that a key’s hash value is immutable (if the object’s hash value changes, it will be in the wrong hash bucket).
```

Example of potential confusion due to mutability.

```python
>>> f = furl('http://google.com/')
>>> d = {f: f.url}
>>> f.url, d[f]
('http://google.com/', 'http://google.com/')
>>> f.set(host='yahoo.com')
furl('http://yahoo.com/')
>>> f.url, d[f]
('http://yahoo.com/', 'http://google.com/')
```

The problem is that these are highly dependent on the bank. Every bank has it's own implementation and some banks even have several different implementations which makes it nearly impossible to automatically detect and parse the specific version.

One solution would be to create a processor to take care of this: https://mt940.readthedocs.io/en/latest/mt940.html#module-mt940.processors
I have no objections to accepting processor pull requests so people can enable this type of processing but enabling it by default is probably not feasible. They're fairly easy to use though: https://github.com/WoLpH/mt940/blob/develop/tests/test_processors.py#L56-L64

I think it's impossible to do it correctly and you'll have to start guessing somewhere.

When I download 3 years of mt940 the file will be:

:60F:C160101...
:61:150303 0330 ....
:61:140303 0330 ....
:61:130303 0330 ....
:62F:C130101...

So start: 01-01-2013, end: 01-01-2016 and 3 transactions, which have value-date of 3-3-[2013,2014,2015]. They all have a transaction date of March 30. I think it's possible to know if the 3rd transaction has a transaction date of 30-3-2013 or 30-3-2014.

Let's hope the mt940 disappears soon in favor of CAMT053 :-(

I wrote a quick char map for the a00 variant, only to find your much more robust version based on the codec package. I may have some data points that can help you. (and would be willing to help after you/we/others decide what values/chars map as what.)

* 0xe7, ea, f0, f1, f9: from the datasheet, these look like g, j, p, q, y (respectively) such that their tails are in the bottom 2 dot rows. I haven't verified this on my LCD to see if they are ignored or displayed raised on 5x8 dot panels. I have not found the UTF codes for bottom aligned/raised g, j, p, q, y to use in 0x67, 6a, 70, 79. But I don't know how much I care about accuracy here since encoding g as 0xe7 is unintuitive.
* 0xe9 is superscript-neg-1, which I can't find as a single width combo-char, so I use \u207b\u00b9.
* 0xf8, it is x-bar \u0078\u0304.

Other UTF values that should perhaps be encode-able:
* 0xeb alternative could be superscript-x \u02e3.
* 0xed alternative could be the Pounds Sterling symbol \u00a3.
* 0xf6 alternative could be a variant of cap-sigma \u2211.
* 0xff alternative could be a taller block \u2588.

Somethings I noticed about the Japanese mappings you currently have:
* [0xa0](https://github.com/dbrgn/RPLCD/blob/codec/RPLCD/codecs/hd44780_a00.py#L178)
Given how this is just before the kana characters, I suspect that the original mapping is actually intended to represent the Japanese space character \u3000.
* [0xde](https://github.com/dbrgn/RPLCD/blob/codec/RPLCD/codecs/hd44780_a00.py#L243)
This is technically correct for decoding hitachi bytes to UTF, the character rarely doesn't modify the previous character. But I don't think this is what we want for encoding. It is one of those UTF chars that modify the previous char, such that they both only take up a single character width. On the LCD, they can't share a character and should each have its own char. The stand-alone version is \u309b. I do not know if there is a 1/2 width version of this character. My vote would be for consistency to mimic what is on the LCD, and I would decode 0xde to \u309b. But allow both \u309b and \uffdf to encode to 0xde.
* [0xdf](https://github.com/dbrgn/RPLCD/blob/codec/RPLCD/codecs/hd44780_a00.py#L244)
Same as L243, but for \u309c. Additionally, there is very very visually similar \u00b0.

I think the cat detection code didn't use Sharpen/Emboss/EdgeDetect/DirectedEdgeDetect. These all use a pickled function, so they can't easily be used with multiprocessing (i.e. spawning background processes). The library has stuff for background augmentation, which rely on multiprocessing. Basically if you use `BackgroundAugmenter` or `.augment_batches(..., background=True)` then you use the library's background functionality. I suspect that this is also the case when using keras with generator functions. I vaguely recall that they use `multiprocessing.pool` to load batches from the generator, which would then cause the same pickle error. Maybe that can be deactivated in keras? Otherwise you have to write a loop, so that you avoid using a generator for keras.

I could imagine a scenario where the same kernel is used to run multiple coroutines submitted to it.  So, for that case, I'm not sure it makes sense to shutdown all of the resources upon completion of each run() call.   I could also envision a situation where there are some background (daemon) tasks sitting there waiting to do things on each run() invocation--and you wouldn't want to have them shutdown.

Let me play around with it.  In thinking about this problem, I realize that there is a very subtle bug in the whole shutdown process that could cause the kernel to spin indefinitely if SystemExit or KeyboardInterrupt is raised as described in this bug.   So, I'd like to fix that too.

Would add:  If there is the concept of an "Asynchronous Generator", the issue of how to handle GeneratorExit on close() is going to be a bigger issue than curio.  For example, are you simply not allowed to perform any async operation at all in a finally block?     Yikes.   I could see that being a big can of worms.

To expand on my last comment, there are basically two possibilities:

* You implement a custom fix for your use case
This can be achieved with inheritance, for example (note that this version would run the callbacks within the locked critical section)

```python
class ThreadSafeFysom(Fysom):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        Fysom.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)

    def _build_event(self, event):
        base_event = Fysom._build_event(self, event)
        def threadsafe_event(*args, **kwargs):
            print("Acquiring lock") # replace this with actual locking
            base_event(*args, **kwargs)
            print("Releasing lock!") # replace this with actual locking

        return threadsafe_event
```

* We try to find a generic solution that fits in the fysom library itself, making your job easier.

>  I run into issues when running the mibCompiler.compile() method and it looks like it is trying to generate code for the ASN mibs before it loads the precompiled MIBs from the list of borrowers.

The compiler never loads up the *.py files, it may only produce them.

The way compiler operates is that it parses the MIB you give it plus all its dependencies into AST, then pass that AST into symbol table [pseudo] code generator which basically resolves and builds up all the dependent MIB objects.

Past this step you end up with the AST holding fully resolved MIB obejcts you can pass to regular code generator(s) to produce whatever text you want out of it.

The *borrower* thing can only pull in a substitute end product (e.g. compiled MIB) which could not be produced in a normal way (along the lines explained above) most likely because of a severe parser error. The MIBs being offered for *borrowing* are produced after manually fixing ASN.1 sources of the troubling MIBs and compiling them.

> Is there a way to get the precompiled MIBs like "SNMPv2-SMI" that live as a .py in my site-packages folder into the symbolTable of the mibCompiler before the code gen process is run on each of the custom text MIBs?

All MIBs should end up in the symbolTable before any codegeneration happens. Including *SNMPv2-SMI*. You should be able to see them all being compiled once pysmi debugging is on:

    from pysmi import debug

    debug.setLogger(debug.Debug('all'))

>  Or does the MibCompiler require that the ASN sources for all dependencies are present?

Absolutely, it does! If you point it to the [online MIB repo](http://mibs.snmplabs.com/asn1/), you should get them all resolved automatically.

Hi @meriadok, the easiest way is to call the `to_dict()` method of the object, eg:

```python
for ticket in zenpy_client.tickets():
    print(ticket.to_dict())
```
If the object you want to print contains nested objects, you can try the following:

```python
from lib.api import ZenpyObjectEncoder
for ticket in zenpy_client.tickets():
    print(json.dumps(ticket, cls=ZenpyObjectEncoder))
```
This will recursively de-serialize all the nested objects to JSON.

> What is the issue that this pull request solves?

The primary motivation is C++11 conformance. Switching to `noexcept` now is a preemptive change. I can understand if you don't want to merge this pull request until compilers actually starts warning you about using the deprecated `throw` specifier. Personally, I prefer to change as soon as possible so that future users never get such a warning. As far as I can tell, the reason to use the exception specifier in the first place was to remove warnings introduced by (partly) C++11-conformant compilers (e.g., MSVC14) due to destructors suddenly becoming non-throwing by default.

Also, `throw(...)` introduces a [run-time exception check](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13841559/deprecated-throw-list-in-c11) against the given list of exceptions (the `...` part). `noexcept` is dynamically checked as well but the check is binary (throwing or non-throwing) resulting in minimal branching (and better performance).

Note that all exception specifiers (i.e., both `throw` and `noexcept`) are part of the functions signature. Changing from `throw` to `noexcept` is a change of signature and thus a change of ABI. However, this is not a problem for dlib since everything is header only.

Hi. I've never worked with EncryptedAssertions before, but from what I gather it's an [xmlenc](https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlenc-core/) envelope around the payload with the actual signed assertion XML.

SignXML was written to support verification of SAML2 assertions, so it makes sense to add a feature to support the decryption of xmlenc encoded assertions.

Here is a useful record of a similar effort that ruby-saml had to go through: https://github.com/onelogin/ruby-saml/issues/9 - although they are not finished, they seem to have a working branch, and I expect there will be lots of good practical pointers in there.
My pleasure!

Actually, after reading the spec a bit more, I think we should use the following API: `xmldsig(data).sign(key=key, key_info=<etree.Element>)`. It should be the responsibility of the caller to prepare the key_info XML element, and `sign()` should verify the passed value against the schema.

Here is the while loop:

```
593         while count <= 10 and not prompt:
594             prompt = self.read_channel().strip()
595             if prompt:
596                 if debug:
597                     print("prompt2a: {}".format(repr(prompt)))
598                     print("prompt2b: {}".format(prompt))
599                 if self.ansi_escape_codes:
600                     prompt = self.strip_ansi_escape_codes(prompt).strip()
601             else:
602                 self.write_channel("\n")
603                 time.sleep(delay_factor * .1)
604             count += 1
```

It will loop eleven times each time sending a newline and waiting .1 seconds. If your prompt comes back as empty (for all of this) that means you never got any data back for the eleven newlines and 1.1 seconds of waiting.

You can modify the behavior without modifying the code by setting global_delay_factor in the Netmiko ConnectHandler call. Default value of this is 1. So setting it to 2 will double the delay; 4 will quadruple the delay.

Is there anything you see when you manually login that indicates this device is slow to authenticate?

It would be interesting to know if you pass using a:

```
time.sleep(delay_factor * .2)
```

Or possibly .3 / .4 i.e. do we need just a little more delay or a lot more delay.

Have you tried playing with the `global_delay_factor` value at all to compensate for the possibility of your connection being slow? @ktbyers [wrote an excellent article](https://pynet.twb-tech.com/blog/automation/netmiko-what-is-done.html) yesterday about what the `global_delay_factor` value does and how it can work with slow connections.

Looking again, the reason you're format works is because of the explicit definition of the hostname again in the matching block, paramiko seems to prefer this definition when expanding: https://github.com/paramiko/paramiko/blob/master/paramiko/config.py#L172-L175

But this does mean that you _have_ to add the hostname definition in ssh_config, for every host, for proxycommand to work with netmiko (worth documenting?) - however this doesn't work in my case and doesn't seem practical on a real network with 100/1000's devices?

Looking at your example, wouldn't the following would work for both, as long as you passed the intended port to netmiko:

```
host 10.10.10.27
  user pyclass
  ProxyCommand ssh jumphost nc %h %p    
```

If the above is correct, my change would break backwords compatibility where the hostnames are being defined in ssh_config in the format given in your example (how many people do this?), we could add a flag to enable this matching (the current style) and then look at changing it in the future.

In any case, we can certainly add some more documentation which may help others

@GGabriele Yes, let's try to get it working with all of the unit test. 

Let me know what unit tests are failing and I can see if I can spot the issue.

There is no `NetMikoAuthError`???

Since it's not possible to insert datetime objects right now, adding an encoder wouldn't break any existing usages. There could be issues decoding, but only if the encoded format clashes with something that someone already uses. Maybe a string like "TinyDate(2015-01-01T01:01:01)" would be sufficiently unique to make sure existing applications continue working.

I think this is something that's worthwhile to have in "core" since it allows simple date fetching methods to be added to TinyDB. This is explained in the manual... though it assumes you're fairly familiar with ssh. From `help(SSHTunnelForwarder)`:
```
        ssh_host_key (str):
            Representation of a line in an OpenSSH-style "known hosts"
            file.

            ``REMOTE GATEWAY``'s key fingerprint will be compared to this
            host key in order to prevent against SSH server spoofing.
            Important when using passwords in order not to accidentally
            do a login attempt to a wrong (perhaps an attacker's) machine
```

You want the `ssh_pkey` parameter, which corresponds to the `-i` parameter of `ssh` and takes a path to the private key.

The deed is done.

It is not at quite the level I want yet. But `suppress` defaults to False, and I can delay releasing a new version.

First, there were a few small bugs that I fixed myself. For example `_winkeyboard.py->process_key` had a path that just `return`ed, without any values, throwing an error on `low_level_keyboard_handler` that expected a pair of values. Then there was the treatment of `left` and `right` on key names, which naively split the key name and took the last word (this breaks horrendously on multi-word keys, like `page up`, which also now clashes with the key `up`).

But those bugs were small and easy to fix.

The next issue is that keys that were not released don't seem to count toward the next hotkey suppression. For example, try suppressing `ctrl+a` and holding `ctrl` while you press `a` repeatedly. The first time it's suppressed correctly, but not the following ones. I'm not sure this is easy to fix, and will almost certainly add delay (you'll have to re-check an arbitrarily long chain of keys before).

The last and largest issue is Linux support of key suppression, but that's another whole can of worms.

Still, good job. That was a highly requested, complicated task, and I loved your FSM implementation with nested-tables. I'm definitely stealing the idea for future projects.

Thank you very much for your contribution xoviat. If you have any issues about how I merged or changed your code, feel free to complain and I'll do my best to appease your worries.

It appears that the scan code is not enough information to distinguish whether the number pad keys are being pressed. The virtual key code is required to determine specifically the difference between the middle keys and the number keys. 

There are several options here and I am willing to contribute. The best option (I think) would probably be to modify the scan code immediately within the queue processor if the vk code determines that the number keys are being pressed. Preferably, they should be modified to be the same scan codes as those that would be on a linux/mac machine for the number pad if possible.

Also, [this project](https://github.com/carolus-rex/pyhook_3000/blob/master/cpyHook.i) is looking kind of dead at this point (which is why I am using keyboard), but it does have some useful code for closing this issue.

There are two reasons how this can happen:

1. aioxmpp has waited for 15 seconds for the stream footer and did not receive it. You can confirm that by adding timestamps to the log or simply checking whether it is actually waiting for 15 seconds before the EOF happens. Then it will brutally close the TCP connection on the client side which may easily confuse the SSL stack; even though I have not yet observed that.

2. The server simply shuts down the TCP stream without sending the stream footer or closing the TLS connection properly first. In that case there’s not much you can do about it.

If it is option 1, there may be something we could do about it, but I don’t regard it as *too* critical at the moment, if everything else gets cleaned up properly and does not hang. If it is option 2, there’s not much we can do except complaining at the server implementation.

I'm not saying that one is superior; they're different quantities that you need in different situations.  You have to decide which one you need for any given application.

All I'm saying is that `p/q` is defined as `p * q.inverse()`.  The reason is that this particular notation makes clear typographically that `p` is on the left and `q` is on the right, leading to a standard order of operations in which the corresponding multiplication (involving the inverse) is in the same order.  [Here is a wikipedia reference](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Division_(mathematics)#Left_and_right_division) supporting my claim; [here is Mathematica's](http://reference.wolfram.com/language/ref/Divide.html.en); I'm sure I could find others in the mathematical literature.

On the other hand, the "p over q" notation is left-right symmetric, so there's no clear choice as to which one should come first in the multiplication.  And since either choice *could* be the correct one for some application, we just don't use that notation for non-commutative division.  (Plus, python code is one-dimensional, so we don't really have that option.)

`ansi.sys` is the name of a 16-bit MS-DOS driver that was probably included in 32-bit versions of Windows due to DOS compatibility.
It essentially works by emulating a terminal in DOS, interpreting the ANSI escape codes that applications write.

You could think of it (very loosely!) as a sort of genericn predecessor of colorama. From my understanding, colorama doesn't use it at all, and instead pokes the Win32 console bits based on what what applications read.

Yes, that is correct. Sublime Text 2 is hard-coded to Python 2.6 (there are some minor details, such as it using the system 2.6 on OS X) and Sublime Text 3 has a custom build of Python 3.3 that is fully self-contained, and contains various patches we've had to apply to work around issues.

We may be adding a new environment at some point during the Sublime Text 3.x lifecycle, but there are thousands of plugins written for Sublime Text 3, and many of the popular packages have shared libraries or compiled `.pyc` files that would break if the minor version was bumped.

Sublime Text 2 is EOL, but Package Control still supports it for now. At some point the PC codebase will drop support for ST2, in which case it would be viable for asn1crypto and oscrypto to also.

Since Sublime Text 3 was released less than a year ago, this project will be supporting Python 3.3 for a long time (easily another 3+ years).

How about this? This is basically a simple dump of the `annots` dict into the XML (from the first page in your example PDF):

```
<pdfxml ...>
  <LTPage ...>
    <LTTextLineHorizontal ...>...</LTTextLineHorizontal>
    <LTTextLineHorizontal ...>...</LTTextLineHorizontal>
    <LTTextLineHorizontal ...>...</LTTextLineHorizontal>
    ...
    <Annot A="{u'S': u'/URI', u'Type': u'/Action', u'URI': u'http://opinions.kycourts.net/sc/2012-SC-000694-DG.pdf'}" BS="{u'W': 0}" F="4" StructParent="4" Subtype="/Link" URI="http://opinions.kycourts.net/sc/2012-SC-000694-DG.pdf" bbox="[123.75, 678.6, 233.58, 692.4]" height="13.8" width="109.83" x0="123.75" x1="233.58" y0="678.6" y1="692.4"/>
    <Annot A="{u'S': u'/URI', u'Type': u'/Action', u'URI': u'http://opinions.kycourts.net/sc/2011-SC-000737-DG.pdf'}" BS="{u'W': 0}" F="4" StructParent="5" Subtype="/Link" URI="http://opinions.kycourts.net/sc/2011-SC-000737-DG.pdf" bbox="[123.75, 361.23, 233.58, 375.03]" height="13.8" width="109.83" x0="123.75" x1="233.58" y0="361.23" y1="375.03"/>
    <Annot A="{u'S': u'/URI', u'Type': u'/Action', u'URI': u'http://opinions.kycourts.net/sc/2012-SC-000599-DG.pdf'}" BS="{u'W': 0}" F="4" StructParent="6" Subtype="/Link" URI="http://opinions.kycourts.net/sc/2012-SC-000599-DG.pdf" bbox="[123.75, 347.43, 233.58, 361.23]" height="13.8" width="109.83" x0="123.75" x1="233.58" y0="347.43" y1="361.23"/>
  </LTPage>
</pdfxml>
```

I'm just modifying the `annots` dict to change `Rect` to `bbox`, insert `width/height/x0/x1/y0/y1`, and pull out `['A']['URI']` as its own attribute for easier searching. Messy, but hopefully this'll make it possible for people to get what they want for any annotation type.

(Of which there are apparently many -- see page 389 et seq. of the spec: http://wwwimages.adobe.com/content/dam/Adobe/en/devnet/pdf/pdfs/PDF32000_2008.pdf)

Note: in this approach you should *not* assume that `Annot` elements will be directly under `LTPage` -- if they fit within a sub-element bbox-wise they'll hopefully-maybe be sorted into there.

I will not be building arbitrary sorting on arbitrary columns. If you want order_by, build an index.

And no, `SORT` will not help here because there is no single set or list object that contains all entities. In fact, there are no lists being used at all, and the only way a set will be created with all entities is if you create the most boring index ever.

This output suggests that the crash is happening during `send`, not `recv`.

The Errno, 10053, corresponds to `errno.ECONNABORTED`. In my case this was due to a host-based antivirus program shutting down the connection.

After working around that, the application crashes on Test Case 6 and one of two things happens:

 1. If I acknowledge the crash popup that Windows gives me quickly enough, the transmission succeeds, and the application reports an error on the next test case when it can't re-connect.
 2. If I don't click the Windows popup quickly enough, I get an Errno 10054, corresponding to `errno.ECONNRESET`. This suggests an error that should be logged by boofuzz.

All of these situations seem to me like something that boofuzz should report as an error in the process under test.

Note: When the application crashes, the process monitor shoudl catch it, but that isn't happening for me right now.

We discussed this issue at the Ann Arbor DES meeting. To summarize: for some purposes, we want to calculate the correlation function binned by transverse separation (r_p) and line-of-sight separation (Pi) between the pairs: xi(r_p, Pi). This statistic is frequently then projected along the line-of-sight, up to some max projection length, leading to w_p(r_p).

TreeCorr does not currently allow this type of binning, but there is a straightforward way to do it using existing functionality. Divide the catalog into many bins of fixed comoving radial width. These bins should be narrow enough such that the desired projection length is several bins wide. (We haven't yet tested know how many bins are required to avoid losing a significant number of pairs due to bin boundaries. Something like 5-10 should be sufficient. In the limit of infinitely narrow bins, you recover the exact result.) You then combine the correlation functions for all pairs of bins that are separated by less than the desired projection length (using ng.finalize).

Why can this type of accumulation not be done for the NN correlation?

This looks like an installation issue, not something related to python 2 vs 3.  It's looking for a gomp library in a strange place.  It has a relative path to it (that's the `@rpath` bit), which isn't right.  It should have an absolute path to something like /usr/lib/libgomp.1.dylib or something along those lines.  This should have been set by the Anaconda compiler/linker.  It's not something the the TreeCorr code does anything with.

How did you install treecorr for the 2.7 python?

```
/anaconda/bin/python2.7 setup.py install
```
it seems to install everything correctly.  In my case, the treecorr package ends up in an egg file, which doesn't seem to be the case for your installation.
```
/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/TreeCorr-3.2.3-py2.7-macosx-10.5-x86_64.egg/
```
So maybe that's a clue.  Also, it isn't trying to use any gomp library:

Hello,

I think you should "restart" the execution in a new environment. It is easy, safe and you can track version number for everything.

You can follow the same URI I provide you a post ago but summarizing:

-Create new environment:
conda create -n MyNEWEnvironment python
-Activate created environment:
source activate MyNEWEnvironment

[At this point you will have a more or less isolated framework for your Python code. Please note that the Python binary you can run once the environment is activated is the Anaconda one, it doesnt matter if your default Python version, prior activating the environment, is 2.7 for example]

-Install/enable packages for this environment: Only the packages installed/enabled will be available in this environment
For example:
conda install -n MyNEWEnvironment pip
conda install -n MyNEWEnvironment pyodbc==3.0.10
....
All you need.
[TIP: you can use always conda list -n MyNEWEnvironment  to check installed packages/version]

Now you are ready to run your Python code into a controlled environment.
First runs will through you a lot of package not available/installed... keep calm and install everything (but minimun) you need.

[TIP: When you activate an environment your Linux prompt will change to allow you see which environment are you in, To exit an environment, simply run source deactivate]


I hope this can allows you to go ahead a little bit more.

I don't know of it being implemented in python-pptx, but I have a pretty-awful implementation that _works for my use case_ - but I should warn you that it may well not work for some of your situations, and is likely to be very buggy!

The code is below, and should be reasonably self-explanatory - but I warn you, it may well fail (and definitely only works for duplicating slides *within* presentations - for copying between presentations you open up a whole other can of worms)


```python
def _get_blank_slide_layout(pres):
    layout_items_count = [len(layout.placeholders) for layout in pres.slide_layouts]
    min_items = min(layout_items_count)
    blank_layout_id = layout_items_count.index(min_items)
    return pres.slide_layouts[blank_layout_id]

def duplicate_slide(pres, index):
    """Duplicate the slide with the given index in pres.

    Adds slide to the end of the presentation"""
    source = pres.slides[index]

    blank_slide_layout = _get_blank_slide_layout(pres)
    dest = pres.slides.add_slide(blank_slide_layout)

    for shp in source.shapes:
        el = shp.element
        newel = copy.deepcopy(el)
        dest.shapes._spTree.insert_element_before(newel, 'p:extLst')

    for key, value in six.iteritems(source.rels):
        # Make sure we don't copy a notesSlide relation as that won't exist
        if not "notesSlide" in value.reltype:
            dest.rels.add_relationship(value.reltype, value._target, value.rId)

    return dest
```

Specifically, it has a `Spec` class that parses strings like the above (`>=1.18,<2`) and can do various logic things to test if a given `Version` falls within a `Spec` or not.

One minor quibble is you can't create Versions to represent release lines (`Version('2.1')` is invalid) and thus can't answer questions like "which release line buckets should this issue-with-a-spec go into?". Hopefully work-around-able or e.g. subclassable.

I reproduced it by accident, and found this thread trying to fix it. The trick (for me) was that you need  Tesseract to write to stderr. `error_string` is split into lines, and then `find` is called on each line. The lines in release 0.1.6 were `bytes` instead of `str`s, so it raises the error msean saw. However, if `error_string` is empty then the `lines` list will empty so the find method is never called, thus no error.

For me, it was writing to stderr because I hadn't linked libjpeg properly when I installed Tensorflow. It therefore had nothing to do with the image.

Hi @qbolec the error you are describing have to do with the locals in python more than the `promise` implementation.

The following example will work:
```python
import concurrent.futures
import time
executor = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=40000);
from promise import Promise

def identity(x):
  time.sleep(1)
  return x
def promise_something(x):
  return Promise.promisify(executor.submit(identity,x));


print Promise.all([ promise_something(x).then((lambda x:lambda result: result*x)(x))  for x in [0,1,2,3]]).get()
```

This example is encapsulating the `x` in a new function, so doesn't get overwritten when the next item in the loop is accessed.

One area where doing this is significantly less efficient is handling address subscriptions.

At present the subscriptions list is just a set of hash168s.  It's easy to convert a hash168 to a bitcoin address.

However, if using the scheme of this issue, then we can see if any of the addresses we're watching have been touched by a mempool update or a new block.  However there is no way to reverse the hash to get the address.

So subscriptions either need to be stored as a map of hash to address, or they need to be stored as a set of addresses, and every subscribed address hash needs to be converted into a hash to find out if a session needs to be notified, each time anything is touched.

The performance implications of the 2nd approach scare me with large subscription lists, so I think the increase in memory cost of subscriptions is the only choice.   My measurements of python memory usage show that cost per sub would rise from 96 bytes to 171 bytes.

Setting Sensor.type to msg.sub_type and Sensor.version to msg.payload when msg.child_id is not 255 will make those attributes contain the wrong information. One might argue that those attributes are not that important. You should be aware of this possible problem though.

I would make a third case, which IMO is better, but will still create a situation with partial data for the Sensor attributes.
```
def _handle_presentation(self, msg):
        """ Processes a presentation message. """
        if msg.child_id == 255:
            # this is a presentation of the sensor platform
            self.add_sensor(msg.node_id)
            self.sensors[msg.node_id].type = msg.sub_type
            self.sensors[msg.node_id].version = msg.payload
            self.alert(msg.node_id)
        elif msg.child_id != 255 and msg.node_id not in self.sensors:
            self.add_sensor(msg.node_id)
            self.alert(msg.node_id)
        if msg.child_id != 255:
            # this is a presentation of a child sensor
            self.sensors[msg.node_id].add_child_sensor(msg.child_id,
                                                       msg.sub_type)
            self.alert(msg.node_id)
```

The best way, I think, is to implement handling of *ack* in this library, and use *ack* in your node sketch in the setup function for all the presentation messages etc. That way you can resend the setup messages from the node to the gateway until the node has gotten acknowledgment that everything is setup correctly on the gateway side.

        raise NotImplementedError("No visitor method was defined for %s." %

An implicit, unnamed node is being created by the zero-or-more rule around spacing:

```
spacing			 = ( comment / EndOfLine / Space )*
```

One way to work around this is to name the implicit node:

```
spacing       = spacing_token*
spacing_token = (comment / EndOfLine / Space)
```

And now declare a ``visit_spacing_token`` member.

Another way is to define a ``generic_visit`` function that does what you want with nodes you don't care about.

Try [JaccardSimilarity](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/stable/conversations.html?highlight=jaccard_similarity#chatterbot.comparisons.JaccardSimilarity) to achieve your approach

> Calculates the similarity of two statements based on the Jaccard index.
> 
> The Jaccard index is composed of a numerator and denominator. In the numerator, we count the number of items that are shared between the sets. In the denominator, we count the total number of items across both sets. Let’s say we define sentences to be equivalent if 50% or more of their tokens are equivalent. Here are two sample sentences:
> 
>     The young cat is hungry. The cat is very hungry.
> 
> When we parse these sentences to remove stopwords, we end up with the following two sets:
> 
>     {young, cat, hungry} {cat, very, hungry}
> 
> In our example above, our intersection is {cat, hungry}, which has count of two. The union of the sets is {young, cat, very, hungry}, which has a count of four. Therefore, our Jaccard similarity index is two divided by four, or 50%. Given our similarity threshold above, we would consider this to be a match.

You are at least partially wrong here :-)

You're right that buildout doesn't try to provide an isolated python. But The only packages buildout knows about are the packages it manages itself (in the local `eggs/` cache folder or a global egg cache if you configured it). Buildout doesn't know about packages you installed system-wide.

So if you depend on numpy, buildout will install numpy for you.

The trick of syseggrecipe is that it **does** look for globally-installed packages. If found, it installs them as a development egg in `development-eggs/`. Where buildout finds it, so that it doesn't need to download it again.

**Question**: the django that you expect buildout to re-use, is that a django you installed globally? "`pip install django`"? Or is it a django from buildout's own egg cache?

I got the same problem and found that using the latest version of MeCab solves the problem.

My environment:

* OS: macOS High Sierra 10.13.3
* Python: 3.6.3
* mecab-python3: 0.7
* MeCab: 0.996 (**BUILT FROM SOURCE** https://github.com/taku910/mecab/commit/3a07c4eefaffb4e7a0690a7f4e5e0263d3ddb8a3)
---

This problem seems to be the same as the one reported in https://github.com/taku910/mecab/issues/5, and it has been solved by https://github.com/taku910/mecab/pull/24 merged in Feb 2016.

 Alhough this problem occurs only in Python 3, it is not a matter of mecab-python3, but it seems to be a matter of memory management of MeCab itself.

Unfortunately, major package managers such as [Homebrew](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/master/Formula/mecab.rb) and [APT](https://packages.debian.org/stable/mecab) currently offer older version of MeCab based on the source in Feb 2013, which can be obtained from Google Drive.

 To avoid this problem without using the workaround mentioned above, you need to build and install MeCab from the latest source on GitHub manually, and then reinstall mecab-python3.

Hello: I know the first beta release of 3.7 is coming any day now so it may be a little late to make a change, but when I read PEP 557 yesterday I instantly thought that this concept should be named a `fieldclass` rather than `dataclass`. Since it is already using the concept of fields etc, it just seems natural.

Just an idea.

Agreed on the "compile time" aspect. That's what I meant when I said we could optimize it.

Agreed on the default value tests, and changing the `__init__` signature for the defaults. I think we need to use some sentinel other than `None`, but I already have one that I use for detecting the absence of a default value in a field(), so I'll use that.

Given:
```
@dataclass
class C:
    i: int = 42
    j: list = [1, 2]
    k: mytype = mytype()
```
Then the `__init__` would look something like:
```
def __init__(self, i=_MISSING, j=_MISSING, k=_MISSING):
    self.i = type(self).i if i is _MISSING else i
    self.j = type(self).j.copy() if j is _MISSING else j
    self.k = copy.copy(type(self).k) if k is _MISSING else k
```
As you show, we could probably do something smarter with the defaults for types we know are immutable like int, str, etc.

I think for now the automatic part is actually easier since it's mostly in one function: `_get_filter_arg`

I should put some more documentation for this, but the `_get_filter_arg` function generates the ffmpeg `-vf ...` argument by going through all of the filter (non-`input`/`output`/etc) nodes. By the time it runs, we already have a representation of the entire graph, and we can ask questions such as "how many inputs and outputs does a particular node have."

To tell if a filter node is used in multiple places, the question becomes "does the filter node have more than one output," and if so it needs a `split`.

The `child_map` dict in `get_args` provides a mapping of all the downstream edges for each node. A filter that's reused will have `len(child_map[node]) > 1`. We'll need to pass `child_map` to `_get_filter_arg` and then put splits in there.. something like this:

```
def _get_filter_arg(filter_nodes, stream_name_map, child_map):
    stream_num = 0
    filter_specs = []
    for node in filter_nodes:
        filter_specs += [_get_filter_spec(stream_num, node, stream_name_map)]
        stream_num += 1

        children = child_map[node]
        if len(children) > 1:
            filter_specs += [_get_split_spec(stream_num, children, stream_name_map)]
            stream_num += len(children)  # one stream-id for each child

    return ';'.join(filter_specs)
```

The tricky thing here is that we need to keep track of stream IDs and we currently assume every node has exactly one output stream ID, and that's not true when we have splits. Let's say we have the graph in the earlier comment above:
```
[0]hflip[v0];
[v0]split[v1][v2];
[v1]vflip[v3];
[v2][v3]concat=n=2[v4]
```

Keeping track of stream IDs is done in `stream_name_map`. As we go through the graph and generate filter specs, we fill in `stream_name_map` saying "this node has such and such stream ID," so the `stream_name_map` is a mapping from `Node` reference to output stream-id string.

It assumes nodes have only a single output stream, and that's going to have to change. Basically it has to become a `(node, child_node) -> stream_id` mapping. Some refactoring will have to be done, but it should be completely self contained in `_run.py`.

This is probably the quickest way to get it up and running since it doesn't require any API changes, but longer term we should figure out a way to support both, as well as potentially other multi-output filters.

I think what should happen is at the beginning of the graph compilation (`get_args`) we should pre-process the graph and unify it so that `splits` are injected into the graph just like any other graph node, and if the user happened to put them in the graph already then it just skips over them. It would also be able to detect incorrectly structured graphs. But I'd say this is a little bit further down on the roadmap.

Reversion will generate a revision every time you save a model, regardless of whether it's changed.

When you save a model in the admin interface, the admin interface generates a change message which would normally be saved to the AdminLog model. When you're using VersionAdmin, reversion also adds this change message to the comments field of the revision. This makes it easier for your admin users to see what's being changed.

You can prevent reversion from saving duplicate revisions by setting ignore_duplicate_revisions to True in your admin definition:

https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/wiki/Admin-integration

This adds a bit of overhead to your save, as reversion has to load up the old revision and compare it to the new. Nevertheless, some people like to use it to keep admin cruft down.

I can't tell for sure what the actual reason is, but I suspect that the following steps could lead to the error in question:
1. catchlog hook wrapper creates a handler before running the first test case
2. catchlog [attaches](https://github.com/eisensheng/pytest-catchlog/blob/1.2.2/pytest_catchlog.py#L48) that newly create handler to the root logger
3. the test case mokey patches the `root.handlers` list holding our handler
4. catchlog attempts to [detach](https://github.com/eisensheng/pytest-catchlog/blob/1.2.2/pytest_catchlog.py#L52) its handler, but it's not on the list, i.e. nothing happens
5. catchlog closes the handler
6. monkeypatch plugin restores the original value of the `root.handlers` as part of its cleanup routine

At this point we've got the _closed_ handler leaked through the `root.handlers` list. And it just complains about a closed underlying stream on the very next log call.

We could add a (rather dirty, IMHO) workaround, that would remove the handler from the very list it was originally attached to, but just doesn't feel to be the right solution...
Out of curiosity, what is the exact problem you're solving by monkey patching the root logger?

The problem with this pull request is that it changes what users expect from the Apache NCSA log format and it possibly breaks their log file analysis. That's not something that users expect.

Why not create a custom named "formatter" that includes this piece of information by adding it as a @staticmethod just as it's the case with `format_with_response_time`? In addition it should employ `-` as a placeholder, in case the REQUEST_ID is not found in the environ dictionary. This could make it a bit more compatible with the Apache conventions and the `%L` statement of [Apache's mod_log_config custom logs format](http://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_log_config.html#formats):

```python
class ApacheFormatters(object):

    [...]

    @staticmethod
    def format_with_request_id(status_code, environ, content_length, **kwargs):
        """
        Log the WSGI REQUEST_ID too.
        The request id can be inserted with wsgi-request-id:
        https://pypi.python.org/pypi/wsgi-request-id/
        """
        log_line = ApacheFormatters.format_NCSA_log(status_code, environ, content_length)
        log_line += ' ' + environ.get('REQUEST_ID', '-')
        return  log_line
```

This formatter could then be used by initializing your logging in the appropriate way:

```python
handlers = [ TimedRotatingFileHandler('access.log', 'd', 7) , ]
loggingapp = WSGILogger(application, handlers, ApacheFormatters.format_with_request_id)
```
This **code is untested yet**, but I think this is the way to go without breaking others' setups.

The open question to me is: Do you want the response time to be included in this new output format or not?

What do you think?

> does pytest auto discover tests outside the lib?

yes, it does. It currently searches in Lib/booleanOperations and in an external tests/ folder.
See the `testpaths` key in setup.cfg:
https://github.com/typemytype/booleanOperations/blob/master/setup.cfg#L12

In general I prefer to have the test suite in an external directory instead of inside the library, because it is not necessary at runtime for normal users, only for developers. And pytest can discover external tests just fine. The reason I put `Lib/booleanOperations` in the pytest's testpaths is to be able to discover inline doctests (if any).

It turns out, using the upstream [pyclipper](https://github.com/greginvm/pyclipper) instead of the outdated one that's bundled in booleanOperations appears to fix the issue! 🎯 I'll prepare a pull request soon.

The writer task is not responsible for emptying the queue. At the time this code is executing, the client is using long-polling. The constant polls the client is doing will cause the queue to empty. The idea is that any remaining events generated from long-polling need to be processed before we switch to websocket.

After looking through the Socket.IO code, it seems the way volatile is implemented is in just a single line of code: https://github.com/socketio/socket.io/blob/3b92cc2b2678f0e102d7a882248424475e7116ae/lib/client.js#L147.

This means that when the server is going to send a message, if it finds that the output buffer for the socket is full, instead of blocking and wait for the buffer to have space it just throws away the message.

Your statement:

> Correct me if I'm wrong but I think socket.io keeps track of whether the client has received the message, and setting it to volatile basically removes that check (fire and forget)

then is incorrect. This feature has nothing to do with checking if a message is received. Messages go out on a TCP connection, so all messages sent will be received, the error correction at the TCP level guarantees that. If the server is sending too many messages, then it may decide to skip some, regardless of how well or bad the client can keep up with the stream.

No, that is not currently available. Because Socket.IO supports multiple servers, it is really not easy to find out when a room is empty, since there might be participants in that room distributed among different servers. For that reason there is no detection of empty rooms at this time.

It's just that accessing `Droplet.events` is equivalent to calling `Droplet.get_events()` (except for setting `_events`). It can be confusing becuase you wouldn't expect an attribute to return a new object every time it is accessed. Also people consider attribute access to be cheap, they don't expect it to make calls over the network. A contrived example would be:

    for i in range(len(droplet.events)):
        print("LOG event " + str(i) + str(droplet.events[i]))
        do_something(droplet.events[i])

While this is not very pythonic (you should use `enumerate`) it should be valid code. The problem is more apperent when `droplet.events` is replace with `droplet.get_events()`

    for i in range(len(droplet.get_events())):
        print("LOG event " + str(i) + str(droplet.get_events()[i]))
        do_something(droplet.get_events()[i])

In each iteration get_events() is called twice, this will cause huge slowdowns since get_events can take a few seconds to complete if you have a large amount of events.

The problem is not that obvious in the first example.

I am aware of Python 3 compatibility issues. As far as I know, they are caused by problems in opening pickled objects in Python 2. I will try to resolve that this June.

The setup is a bit complicated (for pip distribution) since there are compiled extensions to Numpy (arc/arc_c_extensions.c). That can be probably resolved. However, another problem is handling of data that is distributed with the package. Normally, packages can be installed in the directory space where ordinary users running Python don't have write access, which prevents directly storing any databases with other package items. Therefore, pip would work, but package would crash if it tries to update database of dipole matrix elements for example. Solution would be to separately install tools (algorithms) via pip, and separately data, in user-writeable location. E.g. package could check on the first use availability of the data, and if it is unavailable, could connect to git to fetch required data. From the long-term perspective of this project, that division would make a lot of sense, as data might grow, and become more diverse. Overall, solution requires quite a few changes, but it should not be impossible in longer term. I will try eventually to sort that, but I cannot guarantee that within next two months.

@dwSun MXNet is built using asynchronous operations. When you load data, run a forward pass or backward pass, the operations are enqueued on the MXNet backend and executed when the parent dependencies are available. 

With your current script, there is no blocking operations, so the training runs through your epoch and keep adding "copy to GPU" operations. These operations don't have parent dependencies and can be executed immediately.  the actual training isn't completed when you reach the end of your epoch loop. After a few epoch you will clog up your GPU memory.

`print(total_train_loss.asscalar()/training_samples)`

`.asscalar()` is `.asnumpy()[0]`, this causes a synchronous operation to copy the memory back to the CPU. When you add this line, the training isn't "slow" it is the normal speed, because every 500 iterations, your network is going to wait for the computation to be completed and return the result to the CPU. If your dataset is small and fit in GPU memory, you can have a `mx.nd.waitall` at the end of your epoch, but that means your entire dataset will be copied to GPU. This makes it pretty fast since at the beginning of each batch the data is already available. However you might run into OOM error, in that case you can for example keep track of your loss using `loss_acc += loss.sum().asscalar()` and forcing a copy to CPU on every batch.

There is a generalization that would be extremely useful for this operator to have. The generalization is very similar to one that was discussed at https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/reshaping-broadcasting-without-hardcoding-target-dimensions/851/6 (you can skip to the last 4 comments, the thread contains an irrelevant proposal although the motivation is relevant).

In short, the generalization would allow only specific dimensions to be copied from the 'other' tensor. For example:

```
input.shape = (1, 2, 1, 3)
other.shape = (5, 6, 7, 8)
output = broadcast_like(input, other, input_axes:(0,2), other_axes:(1,3))
output.shape = (6, 2, 8, 3)
```

In other words, what's happening here is that the you can pick exactly which axes of the other tensor you want to use to "fill in" axes of the input tensor. This is how `broadcast_axes` works, except instead of providing the values via a `size` parameter, you are providing them from specific axes in the `other` tensor.

The reason this is so valuable is that it is common to have another tensor that contains the dimension you want to broadcast amongst a set of irrelevant dimensions. There is simply no other way of "extracting" the relevant dimension from elsewhere in the net, so currently you have to hardcode that dimension into a parameter list, which *forces* expensive workarounds like bucketing where otherwise cheap reshaping would work to make a net that is compatible with multiple sequence lengths, for example. 

The current behavior of `broadcast_like` in the PR would be consistent with this generalization if the default value of `input_axis` is the empty tuple, which means "all axes".

@dbsxdbsx 

I modified slightly your code, adding the `expand_dims` and changing the `concat` dim so that your output is indeed
`(batch, label_num, num_class)`

```python
class Net(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)

        with self.name_scope():
            self.cov1 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=32, kernel_size=(5, 5))
            self.cov2 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=32, kernel_size=(5, 5))
            self.cov3 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=32, kernel_size=(3, 3))

            self.max_pool = gluon.nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2, 2), strides=(1, 1))
            self.avg_pool = gluon.nn.AvgPool2D(pool_size=(2, 2), strides=(1, 1))

            self.flatten = gluon.nn.Flatten()
            self.dense_256 = gluon.nn.Dense(256)
            self.dense_10 = gluon.nn.Dense(10)

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        x = F.relu(self.max_pool(self.cov1(x)))
        x = F.relu(self.max_pool(self.cov2(x)))
        for _ in range(2):
            x = F.relu(self.avg_pool(self.cov3(x)))

        x = self.flatten(x)
        x = F.relu(self.dense_256(x))
        x1 = self.dense_10(x).expand_dims(axis=1)  # for 10 nums
        x2 = self.dense_10(x).expand_dims(axis=1)
        x3 = self.dense_10(x).expand_dims(axis=1)
        x4 = self.dense_10(x).expand_dims(axis=1)
        out1 = F.concat(*[x1, x2, x3, x4], dim=1)
        return out1
```
For example, for `batch=8`, `num_label=4`, `num_classes=10` we indeed get the shape we expect:

output.shape

from zmq import green as zmq
from zmq.utils.monitor import recv_monitor_message
from gevent.pywsgi import WSGIServer

class WSGIServerApplication(object):

    def __init__(self, addr, target_addr):
        ip, port = addr.replace('http://', '').split(':')
        self._addr = (ip, int(port))
        self._context = zmq.context()
        self._sock = self._context.socket(zmq.PUSH)
        self._sock.connect(target_addr)
        self._monitor_sock = self._sock.get_monitor_socket(zmq.EVENT_ALL)
     
     def start_monitoring(e):
         poller = zmq.Poller()

              events = dict(poller.poll(1))
              if self._monitor_sock in events:
                  mon_msg = recv_monitor_message(self._monitor_sock)
                  # do something with msg

    def handle_req(self, environ, start_response):
        req_id = req_id_generator()
        try:
            sock.send_multipart([...., ..., ....])
            start_response('200 OK', [])
            return []
        except Exception:
            logger.exception("error handling request")
            start_response('500 Internal Server Error', [])
            return []
        finally:
            logger.debug("finished request, req-id %s", req_id)


    def serve(self, e):
        s = WSGIServer(self._addr, self.handle_req)
        s.start()
        try:
            e.wait()
        finally:
            s.close()
```

I spent some time looking into this. The test case I'm using looks like this:

```
def test_hang():
    assert (
        linkify("an@email.com<mailto:an@email.com>", parse_email=True) ==
        ''
    )
```

I think there are a few things about this string that are causing problems. First is that there is an email address in the `.text` of the first node. Second is that the email address happens twice, so when `.linkify()` does `re.sub()`, that replaces all the instances of the email address which causes the loops to just keep growing.

We should probably look at passing in `count=` as an argument to `re.sub()`. I don't think that'll fix this, but it seems like it should be just replacing the first one anyhow.

There's a recursion on `.linkify_nodes()` that surprises me since it doesn't reduce the tree at all in the recursion step. That's fishy, too.

Anyhow, I don't want to spend more time on this for 1.5, so I'm going to bump it.

I'm reluctant to support a list of rules based on regular expressions because it then becomes a case of iterating through the list of rules, running the regex and seeing if any match. This could potentially be very bad for performance. Alternatively, you use an event stream processor but that is on another level of complexity.

The simple alternative, as far as I can tell, is to use equality match rules that work as a simple query. Alerta defines many different alert attributes that can be used to group alerts and it is these attributes that I have used to define rules eg. `environment`, `service`, `group`. However, `resource` and `event` attributes are supported for situations that require that level of granularity.

I have also supported the use of `tags` to define a blackout rule which should allow a lot of flexibility -- one or more tags can be required to match an alert for the suppression to apply. And tags can be added at source, using the `alerta` CLI, or using a plug-in.

I have only done the work on the API at present. Feedback on this would be welcome before I start work on adding support to the web UI and CLI tool.
Boo. Looks like the code was removed as part of "Style fixes" https://github.com/jsonpickle/jsonpickle/commit/3e3296bb6ba1a9e06409a3138e2f313a090e3dfb#diff-a407a7c860cf65b3175948a216a0b6f6.

I have a similar problem.

Using the above solution provided by @y0rd4nis I've made the following. Though I don't like it and I'm still open for alternative solutions that are better.
```
from jsonpickle import encode, decode
import json

def to_json(obj):
    return encode(obj)

def from_json(desired_obj, json_str):
    '''
    This essentially seals the py/object info from a passed class
    The big problem with this method is that if the desired_obj has parameters, you'll need pass blanks
    :param desired_obj: instantiated object that we want to
    :param json_str: 
    :return: 
    '''
    object_type = desired_obj.__module__ + "." + desired_obj.__class__.__name__
    json_dict = json.loads(json_str)
    json_dict.update({"py/object":object_type})
    return decode(json.dumps(json_dict))
```

I don't know what `response` is used for here, but side effects in the module body is a bad practice
in general.

You could perform the request lazily, so it only happens when first needed:

```py
 env_file = 'APPNAME.APPSTAGE.env'

_response = None

def _get_response():
    global _response
    if _response is None:
        s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='us-west-1')
       with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as s3_file:
           print("Downloading file...")
           _response = s3.download_file('APPBUCKET', env_file, s3_file.name)
           print("Download complete!")
    return _response
```

As noted in https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/10#issuecomment-262154277 I'm now favouring resolving the imperative-vs-declarative question by offering an imperative `Pipfile.in` (that offers the full flexibility of normal Python code) that is combined with an automation-friendly declarative `Pipfile` format (where regeneration from `Pipfile.in` only modifies entries that were created from `Pipfile.in` in the first place).

That kind of approach should provide all of the advantages of both the imperative and declarative models, while the restrictive approach I suggest here would mainly leave us with the *dis*advantages of both models.

It looks like there's a fallback in launcher.c (around line 199) that enables portability but it's never triggered if an absolute path is provided (which seems to be always - at least I've never seen otherwise). 

If the Python install directory is relocated any executable in Scripts (derived from cli.exe/cli-32.exe or cli-64.exe) fails with (the not very helpful) "failed to create process" Windows error.

Possible Workaround(s):

**If you have files ending in '-script.py' in your Scripts folder:**
Remove the first line from all *-script.py that starts with a shebang.
Steps:
Open a *-script.py (easy_install-script.py, pip-script.py, etc..) in a text editor
On the first line you might see something like this:
`#!C:\<your python install folder>\python.exe`
Remove the line and save the file. 

**If you only have executables in your Scripts folder:**
Edit the executable and replace the existing shebang path with a relative path.
Steps:
Open the executable in a hex or text editor (Notepad++ works fine) . 
Search for  `__main__.py`
The first non-empty line above the first occurrence will end with a shebang path. Replace it with:
`#!%cd%\..\python.exe`
Save the file.

Either of these will force the launcher to reach the fallback code and start correctly. These files will need to be edited again if overwritten or updated.

Additional thoughts:
There may have been a very good reason why the absolute path takes precedence. Maybe someone else can provide an answer. A possible solution would be to add a check in launcher.c so that it looks for the presence of a file in the Scripts directory (say `portable.txt`) and if found immediately jump to the fallback code.

This Pull Request was previously #691 however I've moved the rewrite branch to pypa/virtualenv to make it more official.

This is a major change to the virtualenv code base, and as thus I want to get some sign off and opinions from the other @pypa/virtualenv-developers. I don't consider this idea a forgone conclusion without input from the other core developers.

This rewrites the entirety of virtualenv, this is a "dangerous" thing to do which risks breaking things that have been working and generally complete rewrites are considered a bad idea. However while attempting to fix errors like #694, #671, #666, #650, #672, #687, #674, #648, etc I ran into situation where I believe it's not feasible to fix all of the issues with virtualenv given the current methods it employs. In particular I believe that the way -p is implemented makes it extremely fragile if not impossible to work accurately in all situations.

In particular I believe there are a number of issues, most of which stem from architectural issues with virtualenv itself. The (incomplete) list of issues are:


The -p functionality to restart the script, which is likely installed into one Python's site-packages directory, seems to be fundamentally flawed in a way where it's not reasonable to fix it in all situations.
The previous code relied on a number of hacks in order to implement isolation. This comes from a time when Python had no first class support for isolation and the only way to do it was to implement hacks. This also forces us to maintain a different set of hacks for each target interpreter and sometimes different hacks for different interpreters for different platforms. However since Python 3.3 Python itself has a virtualenv like module called venv. While I do not believe that venv is (or should) be a complete replacement for virtualenv, I do believe that the underlying isolation mechanism is likely to be far more robust given that it comes from the interpreter itself. This also pushes the maintenance burden of implementing the isolation to the people best able to control what the interpreter does.
The code is extremely hard to read, it is not well factored and over time it's only grown more unwieldy and more gnarly.
Over time the code has grown more and more hacks to solve specific problems. A lot of these hacks are not documented or commented at all and it's extremely difficult to determine which hacks apply any longer or which are no longer required.
There is almost a non existent test suite. I have wanted to attempt to write a test suite previously however the way the code is structured it makes it extremely difficult to actually write tests.
The virtualenv.py module attempts to run as a single file, however it can't actually do that because it requires the virtualenv_support directory to exist alongside it. I believe that it's saner and easier to have a single directory virtualenv/ directory which contains everything that needs to exist for an install to work. This also removes giant base64 encoded blobs from needing to be maintained inside of the virtualenv.py file.
The current means of isolation relies on installing a custom patched site.py that we maintain inside of virtualenv. However this site.py misses features from later versions of Python which means that there are subtle differences in how Python behaves from within a virtual environment and without.


In contrast the rewrite is structured in a different way.

In order to still support the -p option without needing to implement the hackish "restart in target interpreter" logic, the rewrite instead (for the "legacy" isolation method) inspects the target environment for any values it needs. It does this by running a subprocess which will shove all of the required values into a dictionary, and then dump that as json. When the virtualenv script pulls these values out it then loads the json and then has all of the values it needs in order to construct the virtual environment without needing to execute itself with the target interpreter.

Given that newer versions of Python has built in isolation the rewrite wants to take advantage of that. It will detect if the target Python has the venv module, and if they do it will execute a short script (via -c) that will do the actual creation of the virtual environment (without any scripts or tools installed). This will also allow alternative implementations (like PyPy) to implement the isolation themselves so that they can ensure that isolation works correctly in their interpreter without needing to maintain a set of instructions inside of virtualenv for handling their interpreter.

The rewrite also attempts to start fresh with none of the additional hacks. This will mean that we'll likely be broken in certain situations and we'll need to determine how the old virtualenv solved that problem and reapply any additional hacks. However this time we can hopefully ensure that we document/comment what those hacks are and why we're doing it. We'll also ideally write a good set of tests for those hacks so that if we ever remove them in the future we'll have a test suite that will tell us if we broke anything by removing them. Another benefit is that since the hacks are (almost?) always related to the legacy isolation mechanism they can all be quarantined to the virtualenv.builders.legacy.LegacyBuilder class instead of leaking all over the code base.

The new code base is also implemented in a much more modular way. Instead of a ball of spaghetti where the implementation of isolation is spread all over the place, the new code uses "Builder" classes which handle creating the actual environments. In addition (thanks to @ionelmc) it utilizes a "flavor" system which abstracts away the differences between Windows and Posix in the builder code.

In addition to the better (imo) layout of the code base, the new code base is also going to mandate that there is 100% unit test coverage at all times. This will help ensure that we continue to work through refactoring and the like in the future.

No matter which isolation method is in use, virtualenv itself will install it's own scripts and install it's own tools (pip and setuptools). This is so we can have consistent scripts and tool versions no matter which isolation mechanism is in play. It also makes it easier to get newer versions of the tooling and scripts without requiring people to update their entire Python interpreter. In other words if the venv isolation mechanism is available, we will only use it to create the isolated environment and nothing else.

The high level overview of what the major differences in capabilities are:



--relocatable no longer is supported as it never worked very well and I don't believe it's possible to actually make it work in all cases. It appears the major use case for this is to avoid re-compiling software, for which I believe Wheels are a better solution.
The virtual environment always contains copies instead of attempting to use symlink. The new site.py file in the rewrite allows us to copy a lot less files over than previously required. Almost the entire size of an empty virtual environment in the new rewrite is the executable files, which are required to be copied no matter what.
Support a clean and simple API for using virtualenv instead of the hackish bootstrap script method that the previous versions used to use.
The deprecated --distribute option is gone.
The --no-pip and --no-setuptools options now operate independently of each other. In addition there are now --setuptools and --pip options which will negate the --no-* options if they were given previously.
Instead of sys.real_prefix we have sys.base_prefix and sys.base_exec_prefix in order to match what the venv module does.
The new code base is tested on Windows using Appveyor.
The new code drops the virtualenv-X.Y entry points which didn't work correctly with Wheel anyways and are unneeded since it doesn't really matter what version of Python virtualenv is installed with.
The new code only uses setuptools in its setup.py and will always use the script wrappers in that situation.


That major things left to do are:



 Write all of the unit tests (mostly done).

 Write all of the functional tests.

 Ensure/fix the legacy isolation mechanism on PyPy (Jython and IronPython too maybe?).

 Fix virtualenv-in-virtualenv (including for environments created pre-rewrite) so that it correctly uses the "real" Python.

 Update the documentation to match the state of the repository now.


There is an open question about how best to handle the transition. If we just merge this and release it then we risk regressing behavior for a lot of people since this is essentially brand new untested code. In the previous Pull Request @ncoghlan suggested releasing it on PyPI as something like virtualenv-rewrite for a period of time and asking people to switch to using that instead. I think that's a good idea and that's how I would plan the migration to go I think.

The workflow I have in my head is:

  1. Try operation
  2. Catch exception we know how to handle
  3. Call `cluster.update()`
  4. Try again

This works great in a single-threaded environment. What's going to be hard is reasoning through where the locks need to be and what can go wrong in the worst cases. In point no. 1 in https://github.com/astropy/pyregion/pull/54#issuecomment-63265572 I expressed the same idea of ``pyregion`` matching DS9 behavior. This looks like a sensible thing to do as long as it is the _correct_ thing to do. Let me provide two examples for and against matching DS9 behavior.

First, let's assume we are dealing with an HST/ACS FLT image (these images contain all kinds of distortions). Let's say we have a region file given in image coordinates. When we load such a file in DS9, DS9 (appears to me) will convert regions to sky coordinates [for the very least we could save regions in DS9 in sky coordinates; in other words we could use DS9 to convert regions from image to sky coordinates]. Now, we could use ``pyregion`` to do the same: load the region file given in image coordinates and then convert it to sky coordinates. Ideally, we would want to match DS9 results because if we don't then there will be a mismatch between the regions produced by DS9 and the ones produced by ``pyregion``.

Now, let me give you an example of a case when _a more accurate_ conversion is more important than matching DS9. Often, observed stars/sources are too faint to be reliably identifiable in an FLT image. In order to increase SNR often multiple FLT images are drizzled ("co-added") onto a common rectified grid. I will denote the obtained image as DRZ. These DRZ images do not contain distortions. Now, because SNR is better in DRZ, I will select regions in this DRZ image and then I will load them (or use them to produce masks) into the FLT image. In this case, it is imperative that world coordinates are correctly converted to pixel coordinates if we want to produce masks as close as possible to the original regions selected in the DRZ image. DS9 would produce inaccurate results in this case.

I do not think we can reconcile these two approaches (matching DS9 and producing accurately converted regions). In my opinion it is more important to produce accurate results than matching DS9. In addition, users can control which distortion they leave in a WCS object before passing it to ``pyregion``. This means we will have to document the two usage scenarios and inform them on how to control distortions. On the other hand, if we hard-code ``pyregion`` to match DS9 then there will be no way to "add back" distortions omitted by ``pyregion``.

1. OK, let us start with this definition: a local maximum is a region of constant grey level, for which all neighbours inside the image have a strictly lower grey level. The only uncertainty in this definition is thus what happens with an entirely constant image (I would just say, that in this case, there is no maximum). This is the only unambiguous definition I know. As soon as you start filtering minima, reducing the flat zones, etc. you basically always run into trouble. This functionality is currently implemented in local_maxima, and it works for all data types. 
2. Some people prefer having individual points, which means that they need to shrink the regions to a single point. There is no unambiguous way of doing this, because none of the points of the plateau has in principle a preference. One logical choice would be to take the maximum of the distance function of the plateau (which is the same as the ultimate erosion Yann has mentioned). The problem is that there is no guarantee that this maximum is a single point. So even if we do this, we might need to choose one point among several, all belonging to the local maximum of the distance function. The choice is then arbitrary, but it might still be useful as an option. 
3. The next criterion people often want to see implemented is a contrast criterion. They want to have a way of selecting only those local maxima that are "prominent". This can be formulated in many ways. The morphological approach to this is called the h-maxima, extended maxima (nomenclature is fuzzy) or morphological dynamics.
   - Extended Maxima: technically, we subtract a constant h from the function, and we apply morphological reconstruction, i.e. we dilate the subtracted image under the original function until nothing changes anymore. We then calculate the maxima of the reconstructed image. All maxima that have survived the procedure are of "height" h (or more). BUT: the regions we select now, do not all belong to the local maximum of the initial function. Technically, we can write: f - R_f(f-h) > 0
   - Rather than calculating the local maxima of the reconstructed function, we could also require that the value needs to be larger or equal to h, i.e. we calculate f-R_f(f-h) >= h. That gives us only pixels which were also maxima in the original function. This is what I implemented also in the h_maxima function. BUT: there is a major problem with this, if two local maxima have exactly the same value: the algorithm has no way of deciding between the two and will keep both. This leads to problems for the watershed algorithm. 
   - Morphological Dynamics: here, we assign to each local maximum the minimal grey level that needs to be passed in order to reach a higher maximum. We can then select those maxima that have a dynamic >= h. There is some ambiguity if there are two highest local maxima in which case we would choose one of them as the prominent one, and this choice would - again - be arbitrary. Other than that, the solution is pretty clean: we only have true local maxima of the initial function and can assign to each of them its height, as if we had calculated the h-transform successively for all possible values of h. Technically, this solution is more demanding. I think the best implementation today is to use max trees. I have implemented max trees now for scikit-image, but I have not yet written the tests and doc. The nice thing with this, is that we can implement many nice operators with this. I know I promised this already a while ago :-( ... 
   - Alternatives are to filter the image to remove spurious objects, but I think that such a strategy should not be part of the maxima detection itself. 
4. Some people also want to use distance to other local maxima. This is of course also an ambiguous criterion, as if two or more local maxima are too close to each other, it is unclear which to remove. Even worse, the result depends on the order in which I consider the local maxima, unless I take specific precautions. For instance you can have A and B too close and B and C too close, but A and C at an acceptable distance. If you remove B first, you will keep A and C but if you start with A, you might remove A and C or A and B. 

Other than that, there was the issue with computational efficiency. The local_maxima relies solely on the reconstruction algorithm. It seems that there are some developers who think that the current version is too slow, and they seem to have a better solution. As far as I understand, the actual strategy of maxima detection is not touched by this, and it is rather an improvement of the reconstruction algorithm.

Currently, the approach I have in mind is:

`temporal_memory.py` is a readable implementation that is easy to experiment with.

It turns out the bad performance was mainly due to improper parameters rather than the initialization scheme. I had synPermActiveInc=0.0001 and synPermInactiveDec=0.0005 before: the decrement is 5 times larger than the increment. This was fine with the old initialization scheme which has many strong connections. With the new initialization scheme, I find that the decrement has to be smaller than the increment for SP to learn.

Basically, I found the following combinations work (with similar performance)
* For old initialization scheme, synPermInactiveDec should be greater than synPermActiveInc in order to remove the many redundant connections.
* For the new initialization scheme, synPermInactiveDec should be comparable or smaller than synPermActiveInc for SP to learn.

So we just have to tune the parameters differently with different initialization schemes. As I think more about it, I feel it doesn't make sense to have decrement > increment. If there is large set of inputs (>50), a column has to respond to multiple different inputs. If decrement > increment, it will forget previously learned inputs when learning inputs. I found that the SP ends up with not enough connections after long simulations.
