@breznak - Great to see this come back up. I'd like to see a little different implementation, similar to how we've changed things in nupic. The class that you are adding here has three separate TM implementations even though only one of them is used. Here's my proposal for how to get this same functionality and allow external experimentation with different anomaly detection algorithms and different TM implementations:

- We mirror the `nupic` changes here in `nupic.core`. Specifically, have a raw score function, `AnomalyLikelihood` class, and `AnomalyLikelihoodRegion` class. Clients can use the `AnomalyLikelihood` class directly or through the region in a network.
- We can add an example showing anomaly detection on a data set. We'd probably use the taxi dataset instead of hotgym since hotgym isn't the best example of AD.
- External users can then experiment with the NuPIC algorithms, their own, or combinations. And they can do so directly or through the network api simply by wrapping any of their own algorithms in a `Region`.

One thing I see you adding here that you've done elsewhere as well is try to make a class handle multiple implementations. The problem with this is that the implementations have different interfaces (different constructor params, different methods, etc). A better way to do this is to define the common interface. Then you have separate wrapper classes that implement the interface and translate from the common interface to the specific implementations constructors and methods. Once you've done this, you can create a factory function that takes the common interface parameters and generically creates one of the wrapper instances. The interface may be specific to your application though, in which case we wouldn't put it in NuPIC.

Having spent some time reviewing the options, the best way to proceed with what I have seen is the following:
Package: Angular-gettext (https://angular-gettext.rocketeer.be/)
Benefits:
- Keep .po file to be translated for consistency with Babel
- JS translation file is generated automatically during the JS build step, so only need to maintain one .po file
- No need to change Angular syntax in the jinja templates for the translator to recognise the translatable strings (strings marked with translate attribute)
Disadvantages:
- A slight learning curve due to lack of experience however the online examples are clear
Extra packages required for the build:
- angular-gettext-tools
- angular-gettext-cli
- angular-gettext-loader

Having looked at the JED solution alongside gettext, there seems to be a shallower learning curve for gettext, JED is also quite verbose in its usage.  JED is also no longer developed (except bug fixes) and the developer suggests using a different solution.

here is my workaround, I used this solution in our project (Pseudocode here), it may be helpful for this discussion:

I added this model to my app:

```python
#models.py
class Token(models.Model):
    user= models.ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL)
    token = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    password = models.CharField(max_length=200)
```

then I created my encode handler to add token to my db:

```python
from rest_framework_jwt.utils import jwt_encode_handler
def my_encode_handler(payload):
    token = jwt_encode_handler(payload)
    
    current_password = get_user_model().objects.get(id=payload["user_id"]).password
    token.objects.create(payload["user_id"], token, current_password)

    return token
```

and then here is my decode handler

```python
import jwt
from rest_framework_jwt.utils import jwt_decode_handler
def my_decode_handler(token):
    payload = jwt_decode_handler(token)

    user_id = payload.get("user_id")
    user = get_user_model().objects.get(id=user_id)

    try:
        Token.object.get(
            user=user,
            token=token,
            password=user.password # so if user have changed password they wont match here
        )
        return  payload
    except Token.objects.DoesNotExist:
            msg = 'can not use token'
            raise jwt.ExpiredSignature(msg)
```

so when user changes her password, my_decode_handler will stop login for old tokens
or when they logout i simply pop that token from our db,

You have set up automatic "Github Releases" deployment for Travis. The OSX compiled wheels will now be uploaded to GitHub every time you push a new tag (e.g. see [v0.2.0](https://github.com/google/brotli/releases/tag/v0.2.0)). This is cool, thanks for that! (By the way, Travis currently builds for OS X only; it would be nice to similarly configure AppVeyor with "Github Releases", so that the Windows wheels are also uploaded to GitHub...).

However, the Github Releases and PyPI are two different things which need to be configured separately.

Travis has some built-in support for automatic PyPI deployment -- see help docs:
http://docs.travis-ci.com/user/deployment/pypi/

For AppVeyor, you can define a custom `deploy_script` that calls the 
[twine](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/twine) command to securely authenticate with PyPI and upload the compiled wheels.

I'd say, let's first have both OSX and Windows wheels auto uploaded to GitHub upon tagging. Then we could see how to have them uploaded to PyPI as well. In the meantime, we could just upload them to PyPI manually.

Hi @Jason0812 @copoo,
 sorry for late response. I discussed that with my colleagues and the problems in both your cases are that you are probably trying to use Spark in distributed environment such as YARN.  In that case you need to ensure that data from which you want to create H2OFrames are on some distributed filesystem such as HDFS, because from there all nodes can see the file.

`AirlinesWeatherDemo2` is demo which is supposed to be run in local environment.

So before you start your spark application, be sure that you put all the necessary files on HDFS. They you can create H2OFrame based on URI pointing to the location of the file on HDFS like

``` new H2OFrame(new java.net.URI(hdfs://path/to/the/file))```
Hi @waseem18 how are things progressing with the P3 port?  Super interested to hear how things are progressing.

I think the `sed=None` solution for PSFs makes a lot of sense.  They don't really have a flux in the same sense as galaxies do.  They are a function that preserves the flux of the galaxy, so we've called that flux=1, but that doesn't have any units.  It's not even 1 ADU, the normal units of flux for galaxies.  It's just a dimensionless normalization of the profile.

So for chromatic things where the "flux" (aka SED) has non-trivial units, I think it makes more sense to not give it any SED and just have PSF(lambda) always return an object with dimensionless "flux" = 1.

>  multiplying a Bandpass by an SED no longer yields an SED (photons/nm/cm^2/s != photons/nm).

Does this imply that we should have a new class that encapsulates the concept of photons/nm?  It seems like this is a useful thing to want to calculate, and I thought there were even use cases already where we performed that multiplication...  We want it to have a well-defined return type.

@arunkannawadi , this pull request could use a little attention from you.  Quite a few changes have been made in the WFIRST module, so if you could read through the documentation and the demo to make sure it all seems self-consistent and sane, please do.  You can ask on here if you have any questions.

Also, this comment is awaiting your attention: https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/pull/651/files#r25574020

I did a bit more digging around and I think I have a better feel for the situation.

Astronomers more commonly use photons/sec to define zeropoints, in part because they are typically tying themselves to a particular instrument (e.g., they are using ACS bandpasses, or whatever, and assuming that all observations are with that chosen instrument, which means the only possible variation is exposure time, not collecting area).  So the convention there is that zeropoint is instrument-specific; you could perhaps think of it as a bandpass+instrument zeropoint rather than a bandpass zeropoint. They would find our convention odd/unusual despite the fact that there are clear advantages.  If a word has a common definition, and you redefine it, then no matter how good your logic is, you're going to confuse people, especially those who hadn't read every single line of the documentation.

I still can't think of a solution within our current framework that doesn't result in something kludgy happening somewhere.  So, I think the thing to do is:

- I update the WFIRST module documentation to more clearly indicate what the zeropoints mean and explain how you can convert to instrument-specific zeropoints if you wish.  (There are also some minor code updates because I found another small problem in the getBandpasses routine).

- We open another issue about (a) more broadly reconsidering the question of bandpass- vs. instrumental-zeropoints and the connection to common zeropoint definitions, (b) increasing/fixing documentation and usage examples for zeropoints and drawImage with kwargs such as area (c.f. what Josh mentioned in https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/issues/923#issuecomment-347282467).  Even if we decide to do nothing about (a), I think that (b) is important.

Hi Tai Lee, thanks for the feedback and sorry to hear that pip-accel is giving you trouble!

**Disclaimer:** Let me preface all of this by explaining that when I created pip-accel I didn't intend for it to support editable installations at all (it simply wasn't on my radar). Users requested proper support for this feature and adding it was rather trivial based on integration with older versions of pip. That situation has since changed unfortunately, but I've tried to preserve compatibility with older versions of pip-accel which is why there is now "suboptimal" support for editable installations (I say "suboptimal" because it works fine for some use cases but rather badly for other use cases, as you found out in #71).

**Analysis:** I'm aware of this behavior and the problems it causes (as you describe) and would love to change it but don't really know how to, given the way that pip and pip-accel integrate. I actually (fairly frequently) hit the same annoyance that you describe in your last sentence when I install an editable checkout of one of my projects and pip-accel causes pip to process a multi-hundred-megabyte `.tox` directory that's located inside the checkout. In other words, I feel your frustration and have previously investigated whether it's possible to fix this rather annoying consequence of how pip and pip-accel integrate (unfortunately I didn't come very far, or I would have fixed it already :-)).

Your previous bug report (#71) and this one share the same root cause, however I understand why you reported this as a separate issue; users of pip-accel shouldn't need to know about the internals of the tool at all. I intend to investigate if the behavior described in #71 can be improved and that will likely touch the same root cause and so possibly fix this issue as well.

**Short term workarounds:** In the short term all I can do is suggest a couple of temporary workarounds that may or may not be satisfactory for you, depending on your use case (which I am not familiar with):

Assuming you have access to a requirements file that lists the dependencies of the package/project that you're working on, you can `pip-accel install --requirement $REQ_FILE` and then `pip install --editable $CHECKOUT` to cache your dependencies while sidestepping the issues described here and in #71. Of course this requires that 1) the project provides a requirements file and 2) you have access to the requirements file.

If the project doesn't provide a requirements file but you have write access to or even control over the project, you may consider adding a requirements file and modifying the `setup.py` script to fill in the `install_requires` argument based on the requirements file (to avoid duplication).

Of course I completely understand if these workarounds are going rather far and you may as well consider abandoning your efforts to utilize pip-accel; I'm just trying to think of short term workarounds, I'm not trying to convince you to apply them.

>For that I need a smart mechanism on node, that will be able to track the status of the second node and make the decision to stay master or to switch to standby. I thought that such a mechanism will be Patroni.

Patroni does what you want - tracks the status of the other nodes and makes the decision on whether it is the healthiest one. The reason why you cannot have only two nodes hides behind the mechanism Patroni uses to store the cluster state, such as Etcd.

Considering Etcd, it uses RAFT algorithm to handle concurrent attempts of taking the leader key from several nodes (only one should get it), among other things. That prevents multiple PostgreSQL replicas to become masters at the same time and keeps only one master running in any given cluster.

To resolve concurrent updates RAFT relies on the majority voting. More than half of all nodes need to accept any change before it gets applied by the RAFT leader (we are still talking about Etcd here). If you run your Etcd cluster with just two nodes, RAFT will not allow any writes unless all Etcd nodes are running. If you run Etcd nodes on the same servers as PostgreSQL ones you will not get any HA at all - when PostgreSQL node gets down Etcd goes down along, and the remaining single Etcd node cannot update the leader key. In fact, you will get something that is opposite of an HA - the replica shutdown will put the master into a read-only mode (since one of the two Etcd nodes would disappear and the master won't be able to update the leader lock).

All the above means that you can run HA PostgreSQL with just 2 PostgreSQL nodes, but you need at least 3 Etcd nodes running on different machines to keep RAFT running and resilient to a single Etcd node failure. With 2 DCs you can run 3 Etcd nodes anywhere (assuming that the latency between the DCs is in range of ones or tens of ms), that would protect you from a single Etcd node failure, but not from the downtime of the whole DC. Unfortunately, there is no known way to keep Etcd or any other comparable system (consul, Zookeeper, one-node witness configuration) operational if a DC fails when you have only 2 of them, as all of them at some point or another will require a majority voting.

This is my plan for the release notes on the tag:

```
-   Adding alpha support for `pubsub`!
-   Adding `Blob.reload()` and `Bucket.reload()` for getting / refreshing
    metadata.
-   Adding `Bucket.create()` for inserting a new bucket (equivalent to
    `storage.create_bucket()`).
-   Removing `Bucket.new_blob`.
-   Removing `Bucket.batch` and `Blob.batch` context managers (functionality
    usurped by `storage.Batch`).
-   Make `(Bucket|Blob).exists()` more efficient (using `fields`).
-   Unbundling `project` string in storage `Connection`s.
-   Adding default `project` lazy loading for storage and pubsub.
-   Swapping argument order in `Bucket` constructor to allow
    `Bucket('bucket-name')` in implicit case.
-   Fixing bug where metadata responses in `Blob.upload*` and `Bucket.copy_blob`
    were not being used.
-   Getting system tests (regression) passing in Python 3. Blocked on `httplib2`
    release.
-   Removing `properties=` from `Blob` and `Bucket` constructors.
-   Fixing [bug][1] in reported exceptions for JSON-based (aka Discovery) APIs.
-   Replacing `Bucket.(get|update)_cors` and `Bucket.(get|update)_lifecycle`
    and with properties.
-   Making `Bucket/Blob` getters/setters operator on local data only, rather
    than sending requests. This includes `Bucket.(get|enable|disable)_logging`,
    `Bucket.(configure|disable)_website`, `Bucket.lifecycle_rules` and
    `Bucket.cors`.
-   Removing `Bucket.get_default_object_acl`.
-   Adding method `gcloud.datastore.helper.find_true_dataset_id` which allows
    converting an aliased dataset ID (e.g. `foo`) to an unaliased / true ID
    (e.g. `s~foo`).

To your first question, in general when running on GPUs it's good to partition your kernels to be a multiple of 32 to match warp size, but realistically I don't think it'll impact performance in the average case.  The real benefit is only apparent when using Tensor Cores.  

Padding does sound like it would make sense.  I'd vote we do that.  I'm also starting to lean towards just making this an optional behaviour.

The issue is in `showLearnContent()` within Learn's actions.js. `CORE_SET_PAGE_LOADING` is set to false before by the `recommendedPromise` before the `contentPromise` is resolved.  

There are a few solutions. 
1. Add the `recommendedPromise` into the `ConditionalPromise.all` (I believe the separation was to speed up load time.)
2. Let `CORE_SET_PAGE_LOADING` be set to false only by the `contentPromise` (Assumes the `contentPromise` will always resolve last.)
3. Have a counter so that only the promise that is resolved last can set the `CORE_SET_PAGE_LOADING` to false.

While attempting to manually add a Nuimo using the `bluetoothctl` CLI, I executed the following, in order:
- `scan on` and then `scan off`
- `pair D6:E2:3F:1E:98:2D`
- `connect D6:E2:3F:1E:98:2D`
When the hub is freshly booted and the above commands are executed, the Nuimo is successfully connected to the Hub. However, since the `nuimo_app` isn't used to connect to the Nuimo, the Nuimo can't be used to control anything. 
However, if the Senic Hub Mobile Application is being used to find Nuimos, the `bluetoothctl` CLI  shows abnormal behaviour, and fails to `pair` with the Nuimo or any other BT device. 
These findings crate my opinion that  there may not be any problem with BlueZ (because connecting & pairing works fine when the Senic Hub Application & it's libraries don't interfere, but breaks as soon as the Senic Hub backend makes an attempt to connect to Nuimo)

Also, the `bluetoothctl `CLI responses show that the BT controller is `power off`ed everytime you tap on the `COMPLETE SETUP` button on the frontend, and thus forgets all the Nuimos(& other devices it was connected to). This can be verified by using `devices`command in the CLI, and not finding any devices in the list.

I would generally discourage building into your charm layer source directory.  You end up polluting your clean charm source layer with build artifacts which loses several of the benefits of layering and makes it harder to maintain.

I would also discourage you from rebuilding a generated charm from within the build output directory.  You should instead rebuild from the source layer and into the (separate) build output directory.

That said, #114 should fix this, since even with this pattern being discouraged, the current behavior is incorrect.

Your issue is you're passing parameters to `Client.start`, which isn't working properly for recent versions of Docker. In fact, doing so should display a deprecation warning.

For more info on this, see #822 

To remedy your issue, the proper way to proceed is to provide a host config in `create_container` with a properly set `dns` parameter, as such:

```python
import docker

c = docker.Client(base_url='unix:///var/run/docker.sock',version='1.18')

image_name = 'centos:6'

args = {'tty': True, 'name': 'kyle-test', 'hostname': 'instance-kyle-test', 'environment': {}, 'network_disabled': True, 'mem_limit': 536870912, 'cpu_shares': 1024}

host_config = c.create_host_config(dns=['8.8.8.8', '8.8.4.4'])
ctnr = c.create_container(image_name, host_config=host_config, **args)
c.start(ctnr)
```

> Maybe we can add include paths for search, the way C compilers do.

that's interesting. However in the CPP you have two variants, one for system headers with `#include <file>` that searches in the standard system directories or those with `-I` option; and another `#include "file"` for local headers that are searched for in the directory containing the current including file (and then in the `-I` dirs).

Whereas in feature files, we only have one form of `include (file);` statement, so there's no way to disambiguate whether one wishes to include from search paths, or from current directory.

But it's the meaning of the "current" directory which is the problem (the directory of the UFO or the UFO itself as the directory containing the features.fea).

I'm not sure if this will be possible: `txaio` is designed around being selected at `import` time -- in Autobahn, we have "framework-specific" classes that do the `txaio.use_*()` calls (and also import "generic" classes) so that from a user perspective they either do `from autobahn.twisted import X` or `from autobahn.asyncio import X`.

The other part of the design is to avoid whenever possible creating or returning "wrapping" objects. Unfortunately, there are some asyncio cases where we do this: `FailuedFuture` (because asyncio has nothing like a `Failure`) and for the logger. There are no wrapped objects for Twisted.

All that said, a use-case like your describe is exactly how the loggers are intended to be used (as class-attributes). Autobahn hasn't run into this requirement yet, as you basically have to import from a framework-specific package.

Since the asyncio logger already has a txaio-provided object involved, it wouldn't be too disruptive to delay it doing the "real thing" until `.start_logging` got called but the Twisted side already returns a "real" Twisted logger right away.

However, another option is to structure `spamlib` around the "select framework at import time" philosophy so that e.g. instead of `from spamlib import ApiThing` you'd do either of `from spamlib.twisted import ApiThing` or `from spamlib.asyncio import ApiThing`. This lets your users be blissfully ignorant of `txaio`'s existence entirely -- and they have to select their framework *somehow* anyway (and forcing them to do it via the imports has certain advantages). This also forces you as the library author to "hide" any common functions or classes behind framework-specific packages (which, again, has certain advantages).

Experimental multiple GPU support.  This is working in limited cases and for the `allennlp.models.simple_tagger.SimpleTagger` in the tests, but haven't tested it with the other models.  I have been using it successfully to train other larger models across multiple GPUs and so far it's held up.

I added documentation about multiple GPU support to the PR, but given the partial working state I'd recommend we remove it and leave this undocumented as there are some landmines here to find before this works throughout the library.  A use at your own risk, but not fully supported scenario.

There are several hurdles to overcome before it is fully working.  The largest is due to how torch's `DataParallel` distributes models across the multiple GPUs.  Since torch first makes a copy of the model instance and parameters across the GPUs (using https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/nn/parallel/replicate.py) then runs each on a slice of the input data by calling `forward` any state stored during `forward` (like the  allennlp models for the metrics and the stateful RNNs) needs to be carefully handled to properly support multiple GPU training.  I doubt the current code works properly in these cases.  I also had to include a hacked version of `torch.nn.parallel.data_parallel` to get it to work with our model return types (since torch's version didn't work with our return dicts).

There are also some additional gotchas.  One major one is that when accessing modules added with `add_module`, it is necessary to explicitly call `getattr` in `forward` due to torch voodoo when accessing sub-modules.

BAD:
```
class MyModule(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        self.layers = []
        for k in range(3):
            self.layers.append(torch.Linear(5, 5))
            self.add_module('layer_{}'.format(k), self.layers[-1])
   
    def forward(self, x):
        out = x
        for layer in self.layers:
            out = layer(out)
        return out
```

GOOD:
```
....
    def forward(self, x):
        out = x
        for k in range(3)
            layer = getattr(self, 'layer_{}'.format(k))
            out = layer(out)
        return out
```

Not exactly. `deis pull` will work with any registry that is discoverable from the cluster, so if you have a registry at 192.168.0.10 and your cluster is at 192.168.0.11 through 192.168.0.15, you can just run `deis pull 192.168.0.10/myimage:build-5309` and the controller will pull that image in and promote it as the build image. @GillesVandewiele 
"Do the motifs/subsequences need to occur multiple times EXACTLY in the timeserie or is the shape enough?" - Right now I'm using euclidean distance and the user can put in a distance cutoff of their preference when generating features. So the match need not be exact (exact would be distance zero), and is adjustable to whatever the user wants. The distance metric computation is also an accessible property of the motif extractor so it can be replaced by a user function if desired.

"the motif extraction differs from the shapelet extraction since it only operates using 1 timeserie at a time (just look at patterns that occur multiple times in the same timeserie), while the shapelet extraction requires all (or a subset) of timeseries to calculate information gain etc on" - It depends on how you look at it :)  The shapelets paper is looking at all of the data at once. Keogh in talks describes shapelets as motifs but with input series based on shape descriptors so we can modify as we like.

This actually brings up an interesting point. @MaxBenChrist et al: do the feature extractors get passed chunked data for a single id at a time? I'm getting much better results applying it to the whole data series all at once first so that I can find motifs that are in multiple IDs. Is there a way to get the whole data column first to generate motifs? Then we would use those motifs for each id to do feature extraction.

IMO bad idea. The reason is that the code hash, node and balance still share ~90% of their Merkle tree branch, so it's most practical to consider it as a single item. So I'd prefer keeping the cost of 25000 for state changes that make accounts nonempty for the first time.

@mingwandroid, I think I've solved the problem above. The clue was the fact it was printing out the full URL `https://conda.anaconda.org/msys2` instead of the canonical channel name `msys2`. This was caused by the code including the binstar token into its default channel prefix.
FYI #1727 will be merged within the coming days for the conda `4.1` release that should be ready for Anaconda `2.6`.

We have a python 3.6 built with debugging symbols that @mingwandroid built, in a special channel on anaconda.org.  Conda doesn't have a good way of isolating it right now (the "features" feature is broken), so we've got it relegated to it's own channel.  That's how we finally found the pycosat 0.6.2 problem with py36.  If you ask nicely, you might get @mingwandroid to dump a py27 with debug symbols there for you too.

Conda uses requests, which has full `.netrc` file support.  I'm not sure if you can do it there or not.

We have yet implemented per-channel proxy server specification.  It's something that could happen at some point in the future though.

@ilanschnell `conda.resolve.Package` has two issues:

* It's in the wrong place.  `conda.resolve` is not an immediately obvious place for a model object to be located.  Case in point, I didn't realize it was there until I searched through the code after writing this to see if I had duplicated something.  Thinking in terms of the Python API, things should be placed where they logically fit.  The current implementation seems to be there because it's only used inside `conda.resolve`.
* The current object provides comparison overrides to simplify comparing two packages.  That's useful, but isn't relevant to what this is solving.  As I noted [here][1], the plan is to refactor that code to use `conda.packages.Package` instead of duplicating the code, but that shouldn't stand in the way of this.

Having a `conda.packages` module provides a place to store all of the Python APIs related dealing with packages.  Note `from_file` in this PR which adds the ability to load a package from a given file and raises a `CondaException` sub-class when it fails.  Further enhancements that I can think of are loading from a `tar.bz2` file and a `from_package_cache('some_package')` function just off the top of my head.  Neither of those are relevant to this change, however, so I didn't add them.

While investigating on #3459, I found that the behaviour you are reporting here (I'm also affected) is due to the fact that your repo is behind basic http auth. In particular the representation of your package just before resolving the list of the dependent packages is something like:

    `https://<user>:<password>@<url>::spyder-<version>`

In [conda/resolve.py:983](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/e038eca300152e75d7d213a091cee95daf36a922/conda/resolve.py#L983) packages with a "@" in the name are skipped. In fact the list of the packages to be installed computed [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/e038eca300152e75d7d213a091cee95daf36a922/conda/resolve.py#L978) is something like:

    ['sqlite-3.13.0-0.tar.bz2', '@s@sqlite', 'cairo-1.12.18-6.tar.bz2', '@s@cairo', 'libpng-1.6.22-0.tar.bz2', '@s@libpng', ...]

For this your package is cleaned and removed from the list of the packages to be installed.

As a workaround (but I don't know how much robust) I changed the [clean function](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/e038eca300152e75d7d213a091cee95daf36a922/conda/resolve.py#L981) in this way:

    def clean(sol):
        return [q for q in (C.from_index(s) for s in sol)
                if q and q[0] != '!' and not (('@' in q) ^ ('::' in q))]

meaning that the package shouldn't be remove if both `'@'` and `'::'` substrings are in the package name.
I'm exploiting the fact that the name of the package coming from custom channels has `'::'` as separator between the channel and the package name. Probably there is a better way.

I also think that the most robust way to deal with http basic auth is to remove all the authentication parts from the urls and use them only when performing `requests.get` in [`fetch_repodata`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/e038eca300152e75d7d213a091cee95daf36a922/conda/fetch.py#L113) and [`fetch_pkg`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/e038eca300152e75d7d213a091cee95daf36a922/conda/fetch.py#L341), for example overwriting `CondaSession.auth` member. But this is related to #3459. Maybe I can propose a PR soon.

It appears that setting a banner via the eAPI interface on EOS will not put the EOF on a newline implicitly like it forces you do explicitly if you set the banner via the CLI.  This seems a like bug (albeit nitpicky) in EOS, but it is the root cause of what I am seeing in the system tests I wrote.  Here is more detail.

I took pyeapi out of it just to make sure something wasnt going wrong with the methods I wrote.  If you set a banner without a new line like so:

```
>>> from jsonrpclib import Server
>>> eapi = Server("https://eapi:eapi@localhost/command-api")
>>> eapi.runCmds(1, ["enable", "configure terminal", {"cmd":"banner motd", "input":"foo"}])
[{}, {}, {}]
```

Then if you look at the running config on the switch using the CLI you see the following:

```
banner motd
fooEOF
```

If the eAPI call adds an explicit newline at the end of the input value like:

```
>>> eapi.runCmds(1, ["enable", "configure terminal", {"cmd":"banner motd", "input":"foo\n"}])
[{}, {}, {}]
```
The banner looks as expected:
```
banner motd
foo
EOF
```

FWIW, even when the EOF is not on its own line the show commands on the CLI still work as expected.

Since, in the system tests I wrote I was just calling randon_string(), it wasn't adding a newline explicitly. Which was causing the new regex to fail because EOF wasn't on its own line.

I think we have a few options:

1) I can check for a trailing new line in the set_banner method, and if I don't find one add one (that does add an extra newline to show banner output, FWIW).
2) file a bug with EOS and implement the new regex and change my system tests to add a new line explicitly

Let me know which one fits best here for pyeapi.

    'number_of_shards': '5',
    'number_of_replicas': '1',
    'uuid': 'DyR3EguMQlODErb4GTw00g',
    'version': {'created': '6030099'},
    'provided_name': 'regularindex'}}}}
```

Long story short, if you want your user to be able to do `es.info()` you have to give permissions to your role to access monitoring.

This is an issue of python dynamic nature where `"500"` is greater than integers. It is fixed in python 3, (you will get a `TypeError`). In the mean time I see no reason to strictly limit the types of a single parameter where it is absolutely clear from the description that it needs to be a number. Some people might even want to pass in something that is not a built-in number type (like `Decimal` or anything that is orderable with ints).

Sorry, this is just one of the pitfalls of dynamic languages.

Hi @kim0,
There seems to be a couple of things in play here.
I think the correct capitalization of `userName` should be all lower case:
```
"startTask": {
    "commandLine": "/bin/sh -c 'sudo apt-add-repository ppa:ansible/ansible -y && sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y git ansible'",
    "environmentSettings": [],
    "maxTaskRetryCount": 0,
    "resourceFiles": [],
    "userIdentity": {
      "username": "kim0"
    },
    "waitForSuccess": false
},
```
This seems strange given that it's [documented in the API as camel case
](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/batchservice/add-a-task-to-a-job#userIdentity) and indeed a GET request on a pool will return this value as camel case, but the PUT request only seems to accept the value in lower case. Perhaps @itowlson has some insight on this convention?
I agree with Ivan that the CLI should probably raise an error, or display a warning if it encounters attributes it doesn't recognize.

Additionally, if you're using the extended CLI, I've encountered a bug in the handling of pool OS detection that is doing some interesting things with the command line. I will try to publish a bug fix release today.

I can only guess what could be causing your segfault. One thing you may want to check is if your codebase  has any classes deriving from Bullet classes that are overriding virtual functions. If any of those virtual functions have changed their signature since your previous version of Bullet then you could have something that compiles fine but is broken.

There is an easy way to turn those into compiler errors -- just mark all the functions in your code that are supposed to be overriding a Bullet virtual function with the "override" keyword. Then the compiler should give you an error for every such function that doesn't match with something in the parent class.
It's a C++11 keyword though, so you may need to enable it in your compiler.

```python
@pytest.yield_fixture
def redis(loop):
    async def _redis():
        redis = await create_redis(('localhost', 6379))

        await redis.wait_closed()

The more I think of it, the more I notice that the `@preflight` hook won't solve all possible occurrences of this issue. Let's look at the example of a Puppet layer. Puppet modules have to be installed before a higher layer can run `puppet apply`. At first sight, the `@preflight` hook makes this possible. However, the Puppet layer uses the apt layer to install Puppet itself. So the order we want is 

1. Apt layer configures sources
2. Apt layer installs puppet
3. Puppet layer installs sources
4. Puppet layer installs modules
5. top-level layer runs `puppet apply`.

In order to achieve this, we would need the `@preflight` handlers to run in a specific order.

About your issues:

- In the snap layer example, you're basically re-implementing the apt layer, so the solution for the apt layer should work for the snap layer too.
- Charms that use the apt layer and run apt commands without using your api are broken. If the api doesn't provide for all use-cases then we should look at fixing the api. I'd be happy to take a look at the code to figure this out. Maybe we can do a small sprint at the Summit?
- Cory's proposal for adding objects to flags would allow us to create a much better api for the apt layer that eliminates the need for any upper layer to either call apt manually or use the python apt library.

I think it's clear that this kind of stuff is hard, but the `@preflight` handler seems to be a hack that is tied very closely to the technical details of your issue.

I think that in general, the issue is that we don't have a way to instantly invalidate flags when something happens. `apt.installed.x` has to be unset immediately when landscape removes x. `apt.sources.configured` has to be unset immediately when the sources change.
Yeah, you're right (still).  I got myself confused again by thinking of it from the other side (requires, in your example).  Please take a look at the referenced PR.

I actually find it hard to believe that many charms depend on this behaviour.  At least, in my experience the number of charms affected by bad repeated application of hooks in update-status outweighs the number of charms relying on update-status to actually make changes by a large margin.

Update status is an important hook; sensible use of it makes charms a lot easier to work with (i.e. using it to actually update the status message to provide visibility into your charm's decisions).  But it's not like the other hooks, doesn't need to be, and I think we could find and eliminate the cases which rely on it making changes reasonably quickly.

My suggestion is to add a flag to reactive to say "treat update-status like all the other hooks".  It should default to true.  When set to false, it would only run things flagged @hook('update-status').  We could publicise the default value as deprecated (and warn when someone uses charm proof and/or charm build), and at some later date, change the default to false.

Alternatively, we could provide another mechanism, like an update-status.d directory whose contents are run instead of the usual hooks.  If charms want to use them for making changes, they can, and if they only want to make status changes, they don't need to do anything relating to reactive states.

Niceties like what I am proposing are an important difference between developing code for a collaborative open source project and developing research code or code suitable for a technical demo. Creating a reproducible process for new contributors or contributors at other facilities is worth paying an up-front cost; it's the only way to be sustainable. That's the "new value" I am concerned with.

My original question was:

> I notice that the VCR tests were run against a local beamline. It would be better to run them against some reproducible deployment, such as a VM or something that our collaborators at other facilities could stand up on their own. How much trouble would this be to do?

You made the case that indeed it would be a significant amount of trouble:

> In case you are not familiar with Archiver, here is a sequence of required actions: (1) deploy a new Archiver three-tier system (like xf23id-ca ), (2) create a new IOC with simulated “known data” of some PV (like XF:23ID-ID{BPM}Val:PosXS-I), (3) access the Archiver MGMT service for registering the corresponding PV, (4) connect the Archiver Engine with the new IOC for collecting and storing the PV time series into the Archiver backend, (5) connect to the Archiver Retrieval service for accessing the collected time series and (6) finally create the VCR file (including another url name). Will this file with the SAME INFORMATION provide any NEW VALUE for testing the Archiver Retrieval interface? Is this the topic that is preventing the PR from being merged?

In case you didn't know, all-caps is generally read as "yelling" and is good to avoid in online discussions generally, including here.

In any case, if it's a lot of trouble to get something stood up, one could argue that that's all the _more_ reason to provide a clear and streamlined process for doing so, in the context of a collaborative open-source project. Could the effort you describe be captured in a VM image? Launching the Olog is similarly complex, and that's how they handle it.

Yep.  In .NET Framework networking, XML, and cryptography all live in one library (mscorlib).  In .NET Core they're in three separate libraries, and making this method work requires a circle:

* XML needs networking, because (even though we discourage it) DTDs can initiate downloads.
* Networking needs cryptography because TLS/https have X.509 certificates.
* Cryptography needs XML for ToXmlString/FromXmlString.

The easiest link to snip is the ToXmlString/FromXmlString methods.

Technically ToXmlString can be written without XML (like in [.NET Framework's implementation](http://referencesource.microsoft.com/#mscorlib/system/security/cryptography/rsa.cs,fd15a0bacd426550), but FromXmlString requires an XML parser.

My recommendation would be to make a `public static string ToXmlString(this RSAParameters rsaParameters)` method, and a `public static RSAParameters FromXmlString(string xml)` method; borrowing from NetFx and [CoreFx's SignedXml](https://github.com/dotnet/corefx/blob/c88064e2054fca0789511410260906ff5ff0468d/src/System.Security.Cryptography.Xml/src/System/Security/Cryptography/Xml/RSAKeyValue.cs); and to ultimately try moving off of the XML representations.

My preferred answers/thoughts:

1. `pre_dump` and `post_load` shouldn't need to be aware of `many`. I'd always want the library to map them over the collection if I am dumping/loading many. However, in certain instances, `post_dump` and `pre_load`... but possibly in the most common use cases they won't. We could in principle introspect the signatures of those methods to see if they have a `many` parameter, and use that to decide what to do (pass in the list and `many=True` if they have that parameter, or map the function ourselves if the parameter is absent), but this might be too magical.

2. I don't see a huge issue in keeping `accessor` around. There's seldom a good reason to override the default accessor, sure, but I think if you want to be able to serialize both objects and dicts without making the user jump through hoops in `pre_dump`, then there isn't a single consistent format that `pre_dump` is going to spit out.

class MySchema(Schema):

The trailing underscore recommendation in PEP8 is for avoiding conflicts with keywords, e.g. for `class_`, rather than to avoid shadowing built-ins.

I feel like for something like a class attribute, it's not quite so bad, since in practice you don't shadow the built-in except for declaring other class attributes. For example, this doesn't trigger `redefined-builtin` in pylint (flake8's checks don't cover this at all).

@AndrewPashkin `is` would work **sometimes**.  `a is b` means "a occupies the same memory as b", which means that `is` comparisons wouldn't work between enum members that were unpickled by different calls to `load`.  For example, if you have a pickled object containing values from an Enum and you unpickle that object twice, you'll get two different copies of the Enum, which means `is` comparisons between the two copies of the Enum won't work as expected.

It looks like you are all set, except for one thing. Each cell in the `annotations` column must contain a list of dictionaries. This is to allow more than one object per image. Of course, if you are only planning to have one object per image, that is perfectly fine too and I have trained successful detectors on such data. However, we still need it to be a list in that case. You can correct this for your data by calling:

```python
data['annotations'] = data['annotations'].apply(lambda x: [x])
```

This will put what you have now into lists of length 1 and the detector functions should be happy.

Of course, we will look into loompy 2! We will gradually improve the interface with anndata and converge to something that is mature with clear and powerful conventions.

- Regarding sparse data: it's not so much about CSC vs. COO but about a unique definition of attributes that enable the definition of a "SparseDataset" class that allows treating sparse data like a usual h5py Dataset (see [here](https://github.com/theislab/anndata/blob/19cd249b6898cdf09f280873650bcef8d589e380/anndata/h5py/h5sparse.py#L112-L236) for a draft of the code, see my blog [post](http://falexwolf.de/blog/171212_sparse_matrices_with_h5py/) linked above); I'll also talk to the 10x Genomics developers about this. Appending is only possible in one direction for CSC and CSR. I'm not sure whether this is enough. 10x Genomics currently uses CSC. It's important to not having to load a whole Cell Ranger file into memory just to create a loom or an AnnData backing file.
- With "arbitrary unstructured annotation" I just mean dictionaries, which are simply mapped to HDF5 groups in anndata. Let's see if loompy can do the same - my current understanding is that you use HDF5 group attributes for this purpose.
- With "categorical data type" I mean the pandas categorical [data type](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/categorical.html), very similar to R's "factor". Before, we used integers and strings, too; but the advantages of a proper categorical data type outweighed the disadvantages of having a few lines of additional code for the HDF5 backing and of using pandas as a dependency for AnnData objects. It all boils down to having to arrays: one "codes" of length nr. observations and another "categories" of length nr. categories.

Possible problematic cases:

In `_reply_regexp` near line 2299 when handling optionals in triggers:

```python
            regexp = re.sub(r'\s*\[' + re.escape(match) + '\]\s*',
                '(?:' + pipes + r'|(?:\\s|\\b))', regexp)
```

If the contents of `pipes` can contain any special characters this could break. Interestingly, the `\\b` doesn't raise an exception, and according to the new (3.6) docs, `\\s` should get expanded into a literal space character which probably isn't what we want either (tabs could break it).

Further down near line 2311 when it handles arrays:

```
regexp = re.sub(r'\@' + re.escape(array) + r'\b', rep, regexp)
```

To be safe we could replace this with a standard string replacement.

Possible things to test for breakage with optionals in triggers:

* The user's message should be able to contain tab characters `\t` instead of spaces on either side of an optional and the message should still match (tests the `\s` being replaced with a space problem)
* Do more testing in Python 3.6 in general to see why it likes `\b` as an escape sequence in the `repl` parameter. It might not be doing what I expect.
* Turn off strict mode, turn on UTF-8 mode, and try to break it by putting escaped sequences in the literal optional text of a trigger.

All the other instances of `re.sub` replace a pattern with an empty string or a hard-coded string that contains no escape sequences.

Hi Will,

pdc and power_dc are intuitive enough, I think either works.  Appending a 0 or _stc should indicate a value at STC conditions.

Eta is the conventional symbol for efficiency, I’d stick with it.  But I’d add _inv (eta_inv) to indicate the inverter efficiency because eta is also used for cell efficiency (sunlight to current).

I’d use gamma_pdc for the temperature coefficient.  Its conventional to use alpha for current temperature coefficients (e.g., alpha_isc), beta for voltage and gamma for power.  But some literature also use gamma for the diode factor.

Its crucial to note somewhere that in PVWatts gamma_pdc has units of 1/C, not W/C.

I don’t care for irrad_trans or Itr.  However, this is a grey area and there’s no convention I know of.  G is most commonly used for broadband irradiance (e.g., Gpoa) and E for effective irradiance (adjusted for spectral content).  In PVWatts, Itr represents Gpoa reduced by reflections.  I wouldn’t use I for irradiance, so perhaps Gtransmitted?

Cliff

Based on the JSON API spec, relationships are represented by resource identifier objects, which identify an object based solely on type and id: http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-resource-object-linkage

While it would not conform to the spec, if you wanted to represent related objects by a slug instead, there is nothing stopping you from simply using the SlugRelatedField from regular DRF, at least in theory (I haven't tried it myself).

On the other hand, if what you want is to use a slug as the "id" in json api for a model (which is valid under the spec), one option is to [make the slug field your model's primary key](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/models/fields/#primary-key). While django's default `AutoField` primary key is suitable for most uses, there is nothing wrong with using another field as your primary key (though in general it should be a field that isn't going to change, since you might have foreign keys on other models that reference this field).

Yes, most package managers and most systems are pure evil when you're not used to them. We can't make it easy for everybody and I (not we) fully expect package maintainers to jump through some hoops. This is exactly the reason why the core team of Z3 decided not to provide any packages for any systems; that will have to be done by the system experts with most minimal changes to Z3. The latter simply because any change of that flavor is likely to break an assumption that was made by someone else for a different system...

Z3 does not use/require/support automake or cmake, it is not trying to emulate the Linux kernel, and many might call it insane. Your proposed changes are therefore rather system specific from my point of view. If we are going to modify the build scripts to that extent, I'd rather add a general-purpose feature that works for all systems, so that we have to test it only once. For instance, we could think about custom commands after every cp/mv/install. 

However, for the purpose of publishing ports packages, I think all of this is really not necessary; we have scripts/mk_unix_dist.py to generate a unix distribution file which should be more than sufficient.

That's a good point. However, people are much more likely to change the nonlinearity of the layer than the initialization strategy, so I'm not sure if that would be a good idea. The nice thing about keeping the default gain at `1.0` is that it actually works fine for almost all possible nonlinearities, unless you go beyond 20-30 layers. It takes a while for a product of missing gain factors `sqrt(2)` to accumulate into a problematic scaling constant that spans several order of magnitude (`sqrt(2)**20` is only about `10**3`).

In principle you are right though, they are not matched. Unfortunately changing defaults like these is rather difficult, as it is likely to break a lot of people's old code. It would take a very long deprecation period and I'm not sure it's worth it. If we do ever decide to change some of these, I would also advocate for replacing `GlorotUniform` with `Orthogonal`.

Sidenote: I don't think a gain of `1.0` is optimal for the sigmoid nonlinearity. In fact, for any sigmoidal function (including tanh), the optimal gain actually depends on the distribution you sample from. Yann LeCun and colleagues used a "scaled tanh" that incorporated scaling factors to bring the optimal gain to 1.0 in some of their early work, iirc. If you're interested in this topic, there was an interesting discussion on Google+ a while back that a bunch of researchers (including myself) participated in:
first part: https://plus.google.com/+EricBattenberg/posts/f3tPKjo7LFa
second part: https://plus.google.com/+SoumithChintala/posts/RZfdrRQWL6u

@oruebel RE: writing python2/3 compatible codes: I think having some concrete information about the current code base would help in the discussion about the parallel vs compliant codes decision.

I have found the following types of changes necessary in pynwb to gain python2 compatibility:
1) Using requests instead of urllib.request in setup.py
2) Using six.raise_from to overcome “raise Exception from e” syntax
3) Using six.with_metaclass to overcome “class Foo(object, metaclass=Bar)” syntax
4) Using explicit super(Foo, self) instead of super()
5) Using unittest2 instead of unittest to get subtest functionality
6) Working around __prepare__ usage in  class DecExtenderMeta

The only challenging bit here is (6), worked around in https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/pull/60/commits/849b0c1f32e208de6cc9830259b82e49f09f2739

`W_ltR_data = DataIO(data=lt_R_data,compress=True)

The thing is that the above azimuth angles should result in nearly the same azimuth differences. When you pass under the sun as above, the two angles jump, but the relative difference should not.

```
print(suna - sata)
# [  83.60123028 -448.47641828]

ssadiff = np.mod(suna - sata, 360)
ssadiff = np.where(ssadiff > 180, 360 - ssadiff, ssadiff)
print(ssadiff)
# [ 83.60123028  88.47641828]
```

Ahh, I was distracted by your stray percents comment, now I see the question. Basically in this example:

```python
import hyperlink as hl

>>> hl.parse(u'http://example.com/lol%lmao').normalize().to_iri()
URL.from_text(u'http://example.com/lol%25lmao')
```

The question is: "Why isn't `%25` turned back into a `%`"

The simple answer is that `%` is a reserved character in the path, and reserved characters as a rule are not decoded in parts where they are reserved. Other percent-encoded characters are most definitely decoded with `to_iri()`:

```python
>>> print(hl.parse(u'http://example.com/beyoncé').normalize().to_iri().to_text())
http://example.com/beyoncé
```

Another way of looking at it is that hyperlink should never produce a "stray" percent, even when it's obviously stray (because the characters that follow are not hex).

Does that make sense? Maybe we should add a new doc with "URLs" by example, because I think it's really tricky to learn this next-level encoding any other way.

resources. If that means ditching a JDL option, so be it.

Hi,

So the issues is just that the contents of librealsense needs to be in a directory. When you git clone into source, it assumes the repo will be a meta catkin package or contain a catkin package within it. By ROS standards, that's how all repositories should be built, but its a 50/50 if people actually do that. For instance, realsense ROS repository does this correctly and doesnt have any issues (inside the repo, there's a directory that is the project).

So snap packaging has a catkin plugin which assumes that people followed the catkin design guidelines in making projects inside of repositories and not that the repository itself is a project. As a result, when you clone in, the contents of the repo are dumped into source and then there's no directory surrounding it to catch the package.xml file to index it as its the root of the workspace.

The issue isn't as much about snap packaging as it is about catkin. The issue arises because snapcraft assumes compliance.

Hello @jb455 . Several notes/clarifications:
1. The filter that is capable of changing depth dimensions is the Decimation (down-sample) filter which applies proportionally to width and height of the depth image. The decimation factor is 2's exponent in range of [0-4] :
0=> 2^0 => 1 => Output size (x/1, y/1)
1=> 2^1 => 2 => Output size (x/2, y/2)
...
4=> 2^4 => 16 => Output size (x/16, y/16)

2. Can you elaborate the processing flow, as it is not clear, for instance whether you apply the filters before or after align step.

3. We recommend to apply post-processing filters before aligning depth to color to reduce aliasing. This is the way it implemented in ''realsense-viewer''s pointcloud view. 

4. When recalculating coordinates x and y of should be scaled separately as the input data is two-dimensional matrix:
For example : given frame size [16X8] the index of coordinate (2,6)  is (2X16+6) = **38** (indexes are row-major,zero-based).
When down-sampling by factor of 2, the new frame size is [8X4], the new location (1,3) and the coordinate index is 8*1+3=**11**.  And the correlation between 38 and 11 is not straight-forward.
But then again, changing the processing blocks order (grabbing frame->decimation->align) would allow to achieve the same effect while preserving 1:1 mapping in the final stage.

Hello,

I think I've figured out how to do it, so I thought I would describe the process and possibly submit a pull request so other people can get started experimenting. 

The `run_atari.py` and `run_mujoco.py` files will go through the training/learning process and display some running statistics, but it does not save the network's model or parameters when the script completes. Luckily, you can use the `tf.train.Saver` class to do just that. It's as easy as instantiating the saver and then calling the save method with a session and file prefix.

Then, in another file, you'll want to instantiate the same policy (the default is a `MlpPolicy`) with the same parameters (hidden size, hidden layers, etc.). Then, use the saver class to restore the model, and voilá, you can call the act function and get back actions. 

This solution is a little janky and could probably be improved using the `ActWrapper` class that the DQN method uses.

As far as I'm aware, not really. The advantage of the latter is that it can be used when X has not yet been defined, allowing circular references. For example:

```py
class A(ObjectType):
    children = graphene.List(lambda: B)

class B(ObjectType):
    parent = graphene.Field(A)
```

I've also just added this in 3a23c1f940fbd30293b5722c872daa9a86c4c924:

```python
schema = graphene.Schema(name='Cookbook Schema')


class CategoryNode(DjangoNode):
    class Meta:
        model = Category
        filter_fields = ['name', 'ingredients'] # Can now specify filtering on the destination node


class IngredientNode(DjangoNode):
    class Meta:
        model = Ingredient
        filter_fields = ['name', 'notes', 'category']


class Query(ObjectType):
    category = relay.NodeField(CategoryNode)
    all_categories = DjangoFilterConnectionField(CategoryNode)

    ingredient = relay.NodeField(IngredientNode)
    all_ingredients = DjangoFilterConnectionField(IngredientNode)

schema.query = Query
```

Having this option available makes sense to me as the attributes a node can be filtered on are primarily related to the node, not the connection. They can still be specified/overridden at the connection level though.

@GiedriusM, the existing OpenThread APIs should be changed to taking byte-array rather than char-array to avoid the confusion you are raising.  FWIW, the char-array is passed directly as a byte-array to [`mbedtls_ssl_set_hs_ecjpake_password()`](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/third_party/mbedtls/repo.patched/include/mbedtls/ssl.h#L1892), which takes the EC J-PAKE password as binary.

> 1. Should the OpenThread's joiner API take JDC string (and convert it internally) or a pointer to PSKd byte array and it's length as the parameters?

OpenThread's API should take the PSKd - the Thread Certification Harness requires it.  I think OpenThread could provide a utility function that converts from base32-thread to PSKd.

> 2. Is the Thread spec misleading and the maximum PSKd length is 32 bytes and 32*8/5=51.2 bytes for the JDC.

The Thread Spec may be inconsistent.  But I believe the real limit is that the base32-thread encoding is limited to 32 characters.  As a result, the PSKd maximum length should be 20 bytes.

> 3. Is there a base32-thread encoding format reference? For example, what is the padding symbol? Or is the PSKd length limited to multiples of 40 bits, so that it could be expressed with whole-bytes in both base-32 and base-256 (binary) formats.

I'm not aware of a reference, but I agree it would be useful to have.  I also agree that padding needs to be addressed.  We should submit a Thread Group JIRA to address this gap.

Hi @myoldusername,

The learning rate control the how "fast" the model is updated during training: this parameter control the size of the update which is applied to the parameters of the models. Changing the learning rate from 0.025 to 1.0 means that the updates which are applied to the model are 40 times larger. You can read our tutorial (https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/supervised-tutorial.html) to learn more about the different command line options, and their influence.

Best,
Edouard.

Hi @SriniNaga,

Thank you for reporting this issue.

We did not apply any pre-processing on the data before training the language identifier. The motivation is that the pre-processing that should be applied to a piece of text depends on the language (e.g. tokenization is language dependent). For this reason, the language identification is sensitive to punctuation.

One of the issue here is that the text is very short, making the predictions of our model less reliable. We might release in the future a new version of the model which works better on very short texts.

Best,
Edouard.

A note on using the locale in an international setting, e.g. when exchanging CSV files between computers with different locale.
most Europeans use the comma as a decimal indicator, and the semicolon as an item separator e.g. 
1,2;1,3;1,4  
instead of the English version:
1.2,1.3,1.4
Excel for example respects the locale and exports the data correspondingly. That means however, that the resulting CSV file cannot be understood properly when transferred to a computer with a different locale. In my work we often exchange data between computers with different locale, so it is important that the default behavior of the csvkit is the English version, and that the locale is ignored unless explicitly requested differently. At least this should be possible without manipulating the locale.

That issue is due to the commit https://github.com/wireservice/agate-excel/commit/cec58eff2ba9bfddf37214f2390de914e8bd3f3b being missing from agate-excel. When we release csvkit, we'll release all its dependencies at the same time.

On Python 2.7.3, installing the ssl module is no longer sufficient to avoid the exception, because httplib2 has started depending on behaviour that was not present in that release.

So, in my opinion, `httplib2` either needs to handle the lack of `ssl.CertificateError` in 2.7.3, or this error message needs to be explicit about the fact that certificate validation is no longer supported on older versions of Python 2.7.

@wjakob unfortunately I don't know how this works here on github (my projects are on sourceforge). If you don't mind I'll put it here:

```
Inconsistent detection of Python version in CMake and pybind11
==============================================================

The functions ``find_package(PythonInterp)`` and ``find_package(PythonLibs)`` provided by CMake 
are not consistent with detection of Python version in pybind11. If several versions of Python are installed
in the Linux system (i.e. 2.7 and 3.5) the pybind11 defaults to 3.5, while CMake usually defaults to 2.7.
The reason for this design decision is unreliable and buggy behavior of ``find_package(PythonInterp)`` and ``find_package(PythonLibs)``
which are among the worst-designed parts of CMake. We don't want to mimick this behavior in pybind11 in any way.

This difference may cause inconsistencies and errors if *both* mechanisms are used in the same project. Consider the following
Cmake code:

.. code-block:: cmake

    find_package(PythonInterp)
    find_package(PythonLibs)
    find_package(pybind11)

It will detect Python 2.7 and pybind11 will peek it as well.

In contrast this code

.. code-block:: cmake

    find_package(pybind11)
    find_package(PythonInterp)
    find_package(PythonLibs)
    
will detect Python 3.5 for pybind11 and may crash on ``find_package(PythonLibs)`` afterwards.

It is advised to avoid using ``find_package(PythonInterp)`` and ``find_package(PythonLibs)`` from CMake and rely 
on pybind11 in detecting Python version. if this is not possible CMake machinery should be called *before* including pybind11.

```

There is a [really annoying, long-standing bug in cmake](https://cmake.org/Bug/view.php?id=8774) that the `test` target provided by cmake's built-in ctest support (via `enable_testing()`) can't have dependencies, which means if you ran `make test` you wouldn't actually get the necessary rebuild (you have to remember to always run `make && make test`).  CTest's add_test also doesn't add the dependency itself.

As a result, authors stopped using `make test` in favour of adding a `make check` target that does testing (even using ctest) but doesn't have the limitation of the severely broken `test` target that cmake provides on its own.

Now all of that said, we aren't actually using `enable_testing()` anymore, so I think it actually might be possible to re-add a `test` target that just depends on `pytest`.

FWIW, I think that the best solution is just to configure the process' umask appropriately.

If running certbot from a systemd service, stick the line `UMask=0077` into the .service file, which would make the permissions be `0600`.

At the end of the day, setting the process' umask is the standard, configurable, way to control the permissions files created by a process.  It's not a hack, it's the standard Unix administration way of doing it.

Meanwhile, if it set the permissions to 0600 explicitly in the source, then I would have to resort to hacks to get a nice 0640 permission allowing services (nginx, smtpd, et c.) never need root, but just to be a member of the special cert-reading group.

On the other hand, having them be 0644 by default is antagonistic to certbot's goal of being as close to magical and zero-setup as possible, as 0644 is pretty wrong.  Perhaps the best thing to do is to set the umask explicitly at program startup, and add a --umask flag that changes the umask away from the default 0077.

* `pkg_resources.get_distribution` raises something other than `pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound`

So one thing to note is that you need to be serving the domain from the server that you're running the challenge from.
When Certbot initiates the challenge, the Let's Encrypt server does a DNS query for that domain and then tries to validate it at the IP address that it gets back.
So if you have the domain hosted on one server, and then are trying to run the challenge from another it won't work, since it'll make the challenge request to the server that is currently hosting the domain.
To get it to work, you'll need to either request a cert from the server currently hosting the domain, or move the DNS to the new server

Turns out, no, we don't need Trusty in Travis solving current build failures.

The effect of this PR is we change our [build environment](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/ci-environment/#Virtualization-environments) from Ubuntu 14.04 Trusty containers to Ubuntu 12.04 Precise containers for tests that do not require `sudo`. Ubuntu Trusty is still considered in its beta phase for Travis and they have significantly fewer packages whitelisted which was causing [the errors](https://travis-ci.org/certbot/certbot/jobs/173107329#L147) we were seeing. [Travis' advice](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/trusty-ci-environment/#Container-based-with-sudo%3A-false) for dealing with dependencies that are not whitelisted is to either require `sudo` for the build (which significantly increases the build's startup time) or use Precise. Alternatively, we can ask for these packages to be whitelisted, however, I see no real benefit in choosing this route over what I'm proposing here.

This change has no effect on the build environments for tests requiring `sudo` as can be seen in the most recent Travis logs or this [blog post](https://blog.travis-ci.com/2015-08-19-using-docker-on-travis-ci/). If you're using `docker`, Travis runs the test with Ubuntu Trusty.

Just to re-cap:
certbot --cert-name my-cert.org -d b,org,c.net,d.com, e.com will have the effect of deleting a.com, preserving b.org and c.net, and adding d.com and e.com to the cert "my-cert.org". It gives exact control over the domains handled by a cert whose name you know.

certbot --expand -d b,org,c.net,d.com, e.com will look for a cert that has the most domains in common with the list in -d (in this case hopefully it finds my-cert.org) and then makes the same changes (in this example) as the previous --cert-name example.

That is, the difference between --expand and --cert-name is mainly how it finds the cert, and then the domains specified in -d become the new set of domains for the next version of the cert in the lineage.

Redis server would work like this:

1. Redis server starts if no redis server is available.
2. The server writes its information in `~/.sos/redis.conf` so that remote workers know how to connect to it.
3. All processes send and receive runtime information from the redis server.

The problems with this approaches include
1. Persistency, not sure how fault-tolerant a redis server is, but we should not lose any information when the server is killed.
2. Fault-tolerant: what would happen when the redis server is done while the worker still tries to send information?
I would be contented if 1 and 3 are implemented. I'm neutral to have 2.

Also I should add that if you use a shorter CONF then there is no problem. I recall last time we had the similar problem and the reason was the CONF dict has random order and it gets different every time when CONF is large (small CONF perhaps can only have limited random ordering). Not sure if this problem is of similar nature.

This issue is caused by an Invalid version number in the PyPi json information endpoint for streamlink: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/streamlink/json
The offending version field...
```json
"version": "1.14.0rc1"
```
The `version_check` method uses `StrictVersion`, which disallows suffixes such as `rc1` which generates an `Exception`, which is silenced... Even if you change it to `LooseVersion`, which does permit `rc1`, `--version-check` will tell you that the latest version of streamlink is `1.14.0rc1` (lies).

```
[cli][info] A new version of Streamlink (1.14.0rc1) is available!
```

As to why the version is set to `1.14.0rc1`, one of the streamlink team may know and might be able to fix it :)

"""
CIFAR10 DenseNet example. See: http://arxiv.org/abs/1608.06993
Code is developed based on Yuxin Wu's ResNet implementation: https://github.com/ppwwyyxx/tensorpack/tree/master/examples/ResNet
Results using DenseNet (L=40, K=12) on Cifar10 with data augmentation: ~5.77% test error.
Running time:
On one TITAN X GPU (CUDA 7.5 and cudnn 5.1), the code should run ~5iters/s on a batch size 64.
"""

BATCH_SIZE = 64

class Model(ModelDesc):
    def __init__(self, depth):
        super(Model, self).__init__()
        self.N = int((depth - 4)  / 3)
        self.growthRate =12

    def _get_inputs(self):
        return [InputDesc(tf.float32, [None, 32, 32, 3], 'input'),
                InputDesc(tf.int32, [None], 'label')
               ]

    def _build_graph(self, input_vars):
        image, label = input_vars
        image = image / 128.0 - 1

        def conv(name, l, channel, stride):
            return Conv2D(name, l, channel, 3, stride=stride,
                          nl=tf.identity, use_bias=False,
                          W_init=tf.random_normal_initializer(stddev=np.sqrt(2.0/9/channel)))
        def add_layer(name, l):
            shape = l.get_shape().as_list()
            in_channel = shape[3]
            with tf.variable_scope(name) as scope:
                c = BatchNorm('bn1', l)
                c = tf.nn.relu(c)
                c = conv('conv1', c, self.growthRate, 1)
                l = tf.concat([c, l], 3)
            return l

        def add_transition(name, l):
            shape = l.get_shape().as_list()
            in_channel = shape[3]
            with tf.variable_scope(name) as scope:
                l = BatchNorm('bn1', l)
                l = tf.nn.relu(l)
                l = Conv2D('conv1', l, in_channel, 1, stride=1, use_bias=False, nl=tf.nn.relu)
                l = AvgPooling('pool', l, 2)
            return l


        def dense_net(name):
            l = conv('conv0', image, 16, 1)
            with tf.variable_scope('block1') as scope:

                for i in range(self.N):
                    l = add_layer('dense_layer.{}'.format(i), l)
                l = add_transition('transition1', l)

            with tf.variable_scope('block2') as scope:

                for i in range(self.N):
                    l = add_layer('dense_layer.{}'.format(i), l)
                l = add_transition('transition2', l)

            with tf.variable_scope('block3') as scope:

                for i in range(self.N):
                    l = add_layer('dense_layer.{}'.format(i), l)
            l = BatchNorm('bnlast', l)
            l = tf.nn.relu(l)
            l = GlobalAvgPooling('gap', l)
            logits = FullyConnected('linear', l, out_dim=10, nl=tf.identity)

            return logits

        logits = dense_net("dense_net")

        prob = tf.nn.softmax(logits, name='output')

        cost = tf.nn.sparse_softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits=logits, labels=label)
        cost = tf.reduce_mean(cost, name='cross_entropy_loss')

        wrong = prediction_incorrect(logits, label)
        # monitor training error
        add_moving_summary(tf.reduce_mean(wrong, name='train_error'))

        # weight decay on all W
        wd_cost = tf.multiply(1e-4, regularize_cost('.*/W', tf.nn.l2_loss), name='wd_cost')
        add_moving_summary(cost, wd_cost)

        add_param_summary(('.*/W', ['histogram']))   # monitor W
