Thanks Ashoka, it looks good so far. I'm not sure when to add comments as it still seems to be a moving target, and I know Damien already gave you some guidance on integrating it more with the existing code. 

These are a few rough, vague and higher-level notes:

- It still needs some thought to improve how it interacts with drivers: eg. the `extent_upload`is a top-level module, living outside the drivers, but is completely tied to the PostgresDb driver. It's also interacting both with the lowest level api (PostgresDB) and highest level (Datacube) in the same file. We should separate the driver-specific and driver-agnostic parts. Any usage of the high-level api (`Datacube`) prevents it from using these apis due to circular imports (Perhaps replace `dc.find_datasets_lazy` with lower-level `index.datasets.search`?)

- Damien probably already mentioned it, but I'd remove the parts that pass around individual config settings (hostname/password/etc) and use the existing datacube config handling instead. We then also won't need to hard-code dev postgres settings, as people already will have their (dev) settings in `datacube.conf` files.

- The code is creating its own sqlalchemy engine: this might need some cleanup, and if possible use the existing engine setup in PostgresDb (or at least share code with it). It's missing the application name and connection/pool renewal settings and it would be nice not to have to make future connection/alchemy changes in multiple places.

- Currently the pooling/connection handling is disabled, leading to an expensive connection setup (and validation) for almost every query. I ran a test of one month of one product on my laptop and it made ~450 individual connections and ~9400 queries.  Running it a second time (to incrementally update) took significantly longer (I killed it after a few minutes) -- these might be related to #491, though?

- Allow the `extent update` command-line command to take a list of products, and maybe an `--all` option? Most of the time I want to update all of the extents on a system.

- For implementing the tests, there's some existing pytest fixtures that you can use  to test against a real database: have a look at ``integration_tests/index/test_search.py` as an example using them.

- It also might be nice to add a few unit tests for the simple methods with "compute"/util in the . A simple doctest would work on many of them: here's an existing example: (pytest is configured to scan all docstrings for them: [example](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/d951815a6f8b8bda1e95e7691b00360110358ba5/datacube/model/__init__.py#L681))

- Add a test for updating extents of a product with no datasets, and one with no `time` and/or `lat/lon` fields (eg. satellite_telemetry_data).

- It's worth reviewing the API parameter and return types. 
	- Avoid returning sqlalchemy-specific row records: the use of sqlalchemy by the driver should be an implementation detail, not exposed to users. You can wrap a row with `dict()` before returning, or even better use a dataclass/nametuple/etc.
	- The `ranges()` function especially needs improvement: it returns an sqlalchemy row record, for which "bounds" JSONB is returned as a raw string. So I call `bounds()` and then`json.loads(row["bounds"])` to get a dict of top/bottom/left/right fields. (sqlalchemy usually returns jsonb as a dict, but the code is using table reflection rather than your table definitions, so it's not identifying the postgres types properly).
	- Most of our other index apis use a `datacube.model.Range` object for a range, and elsewhere we  sometimes use a two-element tuple. I'd use one of these rather than adding a third representation (separate "time_min" and "time_max" fields)

- the ability to query arbitrary extents is neat! 


A few points I'm unsure about — take with a grain of salt!

- the use of the uuid for extent lookup. Note that it's larger in size than using the fields themselves as a composite (dataset_type_ref is 2 bytes, start date is 4 bytes, offset is char 1 (or 2?) bytes == 8 bytes, vs UUID 16 bytes). It has the downside of being unreadable/obtuse by developers when looking at the table, and is difficult to query/compute in SQL. It presumably may index better though, avoiding a composite index? I haven't tested.

- Storing/using pandas offset aliases as the basis of the api. This seems extremely flexible at the top level querying level, but I'm less sure about storing them at the low level. It's doing exact string matching on "1M" and "1Y", but in theory users could supply lots of other variations ("1m", "1m0d" ... I think?). These strings are stored in Postgres but can't be understood by Postgres  queries themselves (unless we assume we only use "1M" and "1Y"). If we *effectively* always expect month/days, perhaps we could just use a simple enum? Or if flexibility is needed, we could look at a more portable/convertable representation for it: postgres supports iso durations and/or has its own duration type.

- the range `bounds` column is defined as `jsonb` but is always four numbers: left/right/up/down. In lieu of adding Postgis as a dependency, the built-in Postgres `box` type or even a four-element postgres array are simpler and much smaller on disk, assuming we don't need the field to be dynamic(?)

---

Please feel free to come and discuss any of these, especially testing or the drivers/layering!

```python
class Stream:
    def __init__(self, ...):

In such an implementation, each call to emit of each node in the flow path creates a nursery to update its downstreams. When thinking in spacial terms they might be the same thing, especially if you're used to visualize tasks in a tree, but it doesn't translate well to the idea of concurrency (multiple things running at the same time over parallel lines).

Maybe, given our peculiar semantics, it makes more sense to call the nursery a _merge/join/muster point_ and use _splitting/branching/forking/sprouting_ to refer to what now is the `nursery.start` operation.

Oh I see, so it's specifically the "wait for it to be *become* true" part that threw them...

Here's another attempt (based on yours above, plus some inspiration [from the stdlib docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#threading.Event)):

> An event object with an internal boolean flag, representing whether the event has happened yet. The flag is initially False, and the :meth:`wait` method waits until the flag is True. If the flag is already True, then :meth:`wait` returns immediately. (If the event has already happened, there's nothing to wait for.) The :meth:`set` method sets the flag to True, and wakes up any waiters.
>    
> This behavior is useful because it helps avoid race conditions and lost wakeups: it doesn't matter whether :meth:`set` gets called just before or after :meth:`wait`. If you want a lower-level wakeup primitive that doesn't have this protection, consider :class`Condition` or :class:`trio.hazmat.ParkingLot`.

So I'm leaning towards `start` and `start_soon`. This has the nice properties that (a) the one could use accidentally is the one that's marked, (b) in the tutorial we can teach `start_soon` first and it won't look weird and confusing like if the thing we were teaching first was called `start_nowait`, but it does jump out at you so it makes sense to stop and explain what it means.

Regarding the API for tasks reporting back: I did a little more research on the status interfaces that service managers provide.

Systemd has statuses "READY", "RELOADING" (followed by "READY"), "STOPPING", plus you can set a free-form status string with "STATUS=<utf8>".

Windows services have statuses `START_PENDING`, `RUNNING`, `PAUSE_PENDING`, `PAUSED`, `CONTINUE_PENDING`, `STOP_PENDING`, and `STOPPED`. No free-form text though. `START_PENDING` and `CONTINUE_PENDING` both indicate that the service is working on transitioning to `RUNNING` but hasn't gotten there yet.

I think this suggests that even if we want to start with just a "yeah I'm started" method, we should leave the door open to adding more methods like this in the future, both because this is evidence that they might be useful in general (the relationship between a task and its parent is fairly analogous to the relationship between a service and a service manager), and because in particular we might want to pun this interface to directly talk to service managers.

So I'm leaning towards making the kwarg `task_status`, used like:

```python
async def awesome_server(*, task_status=trio.STATUS_IGNORED):
    ...
    task_status.started()
```

(and maybe in the future we will add `task_status.stopping()`, `task_status.status("...")`, etc.)

Including the minimal example from the discussion. The example employs pytest-trio to run this test function with a `MockClock` configured to autojump:

```python
@pytest.mark.trio
async def test_worker_thread_autojump(nursery, autojump_clock):
    def slowish_task():
        for i in range(1000000):
            pass
        return 'foo'

    async def async_run_task(queue):
        result = await trio.run_sync_in_worker_thread(slowish_task)
        await queue.put(result)

    queue = trio.Queue(1)
    nursery.start_soon(async_run_task, queue)
    with trio.fail_after(300):
        assert await queue.get() == 'foo'
```

This test might fail with a `trio.TooSlowError` if `autojump_threshold` is set to 0 (the default for `autojump_clock`) or too small a value. The fail is not deterministic, i.e. in some instances the worker thread could be done running and report back to Trio thread before we `wait_all_tasks_blocked`. Setting a high enough `autojump_threshold` fixed the test (although it still depends on timing, so I guess there might be a very small chance it still fails on occasion).

Tricky part: the code I was exercising through this test didn't make it immediately obvious that it was spawning a worker thread. My expectation was that code that doesn't depend on I/O would interact well with `MockClock`, but synchronizing a thread is in fact a form of I/O crossing Trio boundaries. Unfortunately this is not always obvious unless you inspect the code.

@njsmith mentioned that implicitly waiting for a worker thread happens in Trio itself (in `trio.socket.getaddrinfo()`); although in this case the user might already expect that waiting on the network might be involved (`getaddrinfo` is blocking, and an async interface to it can be expected to be blocked). On the other hand my instance involved relatively trivial operations, with no syscalls, and it was running in the order of a few microsecs.

---

Should `wait_all_tasks_blocked()` (or `MockClock`) wait for all worker threads to be done?

Pros:
- tests like the one in the example would not fail even in their naive formulation
- refactoring to delegate work to a worker thread would not cause a previously passing test to fail
- no need to guess a threshold time, no non-deterministic failures
- the magic combo `pytest-trio` with `autojump_clock` would stay magic ✨ 

Cons:
- tests that expect tasks to block while waiting for a thread would require separate machinery (probably different configuration for `MockClock` or different arguments for `wait_all_tasks_blocked()`) than the ones specified above
- implicit waiting means that tests can unexpectedly become slower
- documenting this fact could increase barrier to access to these tools, cancelling the benefit of having a less surprising behavior
- is `run_sync_in_worker_thread()` special enough to deserve special casing? (*)

The last question (*) makes me wonder whether this issue might indicate that there are different semantics in the general context of "running a function synchronously in a worker thread". Maybe it's up to the implementation to specify that we are spawning a thread with the expectation that its running state is bound to the task that is waiting for it (i.e. it should be opaque to users that we are in fact spawning a thread to do our job).

---

Simpler alternatives: add a note to the documentation for autojump; add a `trio.testing` function to wait for all worker threads to be done.

```

More fundamentally, I see things the other way. The property of the commit is the tree-oid, not the tree. The ability to get the tree directly from the commit is just sugar, a short way to write ``repo[commit.tree_oid]``.

Related to this, I would like every call that returns a git-object to go through a central function. For instance, ``Commit.tree`` should call ``lookup_object``, and not build up the tree object on its own. This way, one day, we may implement a cache at the Python level, for instance.
